Increase **3,727,135** ([2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"))[[1]](#cite_note-Native_Population_2020-1) Increase 1.12% of the total US population • Native Americans: 2,251,699 (0.67%) • [Native Hispanics](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans"): 1,475,436 (0.45%)  **In combination ([multiracial](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans"))** Increase **5,938,923** ([2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"))[[1]](#cite_note-Native_Population_2020-1) Increase 1.79% of the total US population  **Alone or in combination** Increase **9,666,058** ([2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"))[[1]](#cite_note-Native_Population_2020-1) Increase 2.92% of the total US population | |

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Indigenous peoples of the United States

"Native Americans" redirects here. For indigenous Americas more broadly, see [Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas"). For other uses, see [Native Americans (disambiguation)](/wiki/Native_Americans_(disambiguation) "Native Americans (disambiguation)").

**Native Americans** (also called **Indians**, **American Indians**, **First Americans**, and **Indigenous Americans**) are the [Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), particularly of the [lower 48 states](/wiki/Contiguous_United_States "Contiguous United States") and [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"). They may also include any Americans whose origins lie in any of the Indigenous peoples of North or South America. The [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") publishes data about "American Indians and [Alaska Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives")", whom it defines as anyone "having origins in any of the original peoples of North and South America ... and who maintains tribal affiliation or community attachment".[[5]](#cite_note-US_Census_Race_definitions-5) The [census](/wiki/Census "Census") does not, however, enumerate "Native Americans" as such, noting that the latter term can encompass a broader set of groups, e.g. [Native Hawaiians](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians"), which it tabulates separately.[[6]](#cite_note-uscb-6)

The [European colonization of the Americas](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas") from 1492 resulted in a [precipitous decline in the size of the Native American population](/wiki/Population_history_of_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Population history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas") because of [newly introduced diseases](/wiki/Native_American_disease_and_epidemics "Native American disease and epidemics"), including weaponized diseases and biological warfare by colonizers,[[7]](#cite_note-Alibek_2004_pp._3–8-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) [wars](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars"), [ethnic cleansing](/wiki/Ethnic_cleansing "Ethnic cleansing"), and [enslavement](/wiki/Slavery_among_Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Slavery among Native Americans in the United States"). Numerous scholars have classified elements of the colonization process as comprising [genocide against Native Americans](/wiki/Native_American_genocide_in_the_United_States "Native American genocide in the United States"). As part of a policy of [settler colonialism](/wiki/Settler_colonialism "Settler colonialism"), European settlers continued to wage war and perpetrated massacres against Native American peoples, [removed](/wiki/Indian_removal "Indian removal") them from their [ancestral lands](/wiki/Ancestral_lands "Ancestral lands"), and subjected them to [one-sided government treaties](/wiki/List_of_United_States_treaties#U.S.–Native_American_treaties "List of United States treaties") and discriminatory government policies. Into the 20th century, these policies focused on forced [assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation_of_Native_Americans "Cultural assimilation of Native Americans").[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)[[13]](#cite_note-:5-13)[[14]](#cite_note-:4-14)

When the United States was established, Native American tribes were considered semi-independent nations, because they generally lived in communities which were separate from communities of white settlers. The federal government signed treaties at a government-to-government level until the [Indian Appropriations Act of 1871](/wiki/Indian_Appropriations_Act#1871_Act "Indian Appropriations Act") ended recognition of independent Native nations, and started treating them as "domestic dependent nations" subject to applicable federal laws. This law did preserve rights and privileges, including a large degree of [tribal sovereignty](/wiki/Tribal_sovereignty_in_the_United_States "Tribal sovereignty in the United States"). For this reason, many Native American reservations are still independent of state law and the actions of tribal citizens on these reservations are subject only to tribal courts and federal law. The [Indian Citizenship Act](/wiki/Indian_Citizenship_Act "Indian Citizenship Act") of 1924 granted US citizenship to all Native Americans born in the US who had not yet obtained it. This emptied the "Indians not taxed" category established by the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution"), allowed Natives to vote in elections, and extended the [Fourteenth Amendment](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") protections granted to people "subject to the jurisdiction" of the United States. However, some states continued to deny [Native Americans voting rights](/wiki/Native_American_voting_rights "Native American voting rights") for decades. Titles II through VII of the [Civil Rights Act of 1968](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1968 "Civil Rights Act of 1968") comprise the Indian Civil Rights Act, which applies to Native American tribes and makes many but not all of the guarantees of the [U.S. Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights") applicable within the tribes.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Since the 1960s, [Native American self-determination](/wiki/Native_American_self-determination "Native American self-determination") movements have resulted in positive changes to the lives of many Native Americans, though there are still many [contemporary issues faced by them](/wiki/Contemporary_Native_American_issues_in_the_United_States "Contemporary Native American issues in the United States"). Today, there are over five million Native Americans in the US, about 80% of whom live outside reservations. As of 2020, the states with the highest percentage of Native Americans are [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma"), [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), and [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas").[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Shriver_Circle_%26_Mound_City_solstice_sunrise_HRoe_2019sm.jpg/250px-Shriver_Circle_%26_Mound_City_solstice_sunrise_HRoe_2019sm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shriver_Circle_%26_Mound_City_solstice_sunrise_HRoe_2019sm.jpg)

[Shriver Circle Earthworks](/wiki/Shriver_Circle_Earthworks "Shriver Circle Earthworks") and the [Mound City Group](/wiki/Mound_City_Group "Mound City Group") (on the left), c. 200 BCE to c. 500 CE, depicted in a 2019 portrait

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/North_American_cultural_areas.png/250px-North_American_cultural_areas.png)](/wiki/File:North_American_cultural_areas.png)

The [cultural areas](/wiki/Cultural_area "Cultural area") of Indigenous peoples of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") during the [Pre-Columbian era](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_era "Pre-Columbian era"), according to anthropologist [Alfred Kroeber](/wiki/Alfred_Kroeber "Alfred Kroeber")

The history of Native Americans in the United States began tens of thousands of years ago with the [settlement of the Americas](/wiki/Settlement_of_the_Americas "Settlement of the Americas") by the [Paleo-Indians](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians"). The Eurasian migration to the Americas occurred over millennia via [Beringia](/wiki/Beringia "Beringia"), a land bridge between [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") and [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), as early humans spread southward and eastward. Archaeological evidence suggests these migrations began 25,000 years ago and continued until around 12,000 years ago. Some may have arrived even earlier, fishing in kayaks along what is known as the "[Kelp Highway](/wiki/Jon_M._Erlandson#Kelp_Highway_Hypothesis:_The_Peopling_of_the_New_World "Jon M. Erlandson")". Major Paleo-Indian cultures included the [Clovis](/wiki/Clovis_culture "Clovis culture") and [Folsom traditions](/wiki/Folsom_tradition "Folsom tradition"), identified through unique spear points and large-game hunting methods.

Around 8000 BCE, as the climate stabilized, new cultural periods like the [Archaic stage](/wiki/Archaic_period_(North_America) "Archaic period (North America)") arose, during which hunter-gatherer communities developed complex societies. The [Mound Builders](/wiki/Mound_Builders "Mound Builders") created large earthworks, such as at [Watson Brake](/wiki/Watson_Brake "Watson Brake") and [Poverty Point](/wiki/Poverty_Point "Poverty Point"), which date to 3500 BCE and 2200 BCE. By 1000 BCE, Native societies in the [Woodland period](/wiki/Woodland_period "Woodland period") developed advanced social structures and trade networks, with the [Hopewell tradition](/wiki/Hopewell_tradition "Hopewell tradition") connecting the Eastern Woodlands to the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") and the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico"). This period led to the [Mississippian culture](/wiki/Mississippian_culture "Mississippian culture"), with large urban centers like [Cahokia](/wiki/Cahokia "Cahokia")—a city with complex mounds and a population exceeding 20,000 by 1250 CE.

[Ethnographers](/wiki/Ethnography "Ethnography") classify the [Indigenous peoples of North America](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas#North_America "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") into ten cultural areas inhabited by groups who share certain [cultural](/wiki/Cultural "Cultural") traits:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic"), [Subarctic](/wiki/Subarctic "Subarctic"), [Northeastern Woodlands](/wiki/Northeastern_Woodlands "Northeastern Woodlands"), [Southeastern Woodlands](/wiki/Southeastern_Woodlands "Southeastern Woodlands"), [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains"), [Great Basin](/wiki/Great_Basin "Great Basin"), [Northwest Plateau](/wiki/Northwest_Plateau "Northwest Plateau"), [Northwest Coast](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Pacific_Northwest_Coast "Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), and [Southwest](/wiki/Southwest "Southwest") ([Oasisamerica](/wiki/Oasisamerica "Oasisamerica")). At the time of the first contact, Indigenous cultures varied widely, with some Northeastern and Southwestern groups being [matrilineal](/wiki/Matrilineal "Matrilineal") and organized on collective bases unfamiliar to Europeans. Land was typically held communally, in contrast to European [property rights](/wiki/Property_rights "Property rights") traditions. These differences, compounded by shifting alliances and warfare, generated political tension, ethnic violence, and disruption.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Beginning near the end of the 15th century, [migration of Europeans to the Americas](/wiki/Migration_of_Europeans_to_the_Americas "Migration of Europeans to the Americas") led to centuries of population, cultural, and agricultural transfer between [Old](/wiki/Old_World "Old World") and [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") societies, known as the [Columbian exchange](/wiki/Columbian_exchange "Columbian exchange"). Because most Native groups preserved [their histories](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_era "Pre-Columbian era") by means of [oral traditions](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition") and artwork, the [first written accounts of contact were provided by Europeans](/wiki/Protohistory "Protohistory").[[18]](#cite_note-test-18)

Native Americans suffered high fatality rates from [contact with European diseases](/wiki/Native_American_disease_and_epidemics "Native American disease and epidemics") that were new to them, and to which they had no [immunity](/wiki/Immunity_(medical) "Immunity (medical)").[[19]](#cite_note-mann-19) [Smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox_epidemics_in_the_Americas "Smallpox epidemics in the Americas") was especially devastating. Populations in some regions fell by 90 percent or more in the first century after contact.[[20]](#cite_note-denevan-20) [Pre-Columbian population estimates](/wiki/Population_history_of_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Population history of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas") for the area of the modern U.S. range from 2 to over 18 million.[[19]](#cite_note-mann-19)[[20]](#cite_note-denevan-20) By the end of the 18th century, numbers had collapsed to around 600,000 due to disease, warfare, and [genocide](/wiki/Native_American_genocide_in_the_United_States "Native American genocide in the United States").[[21]](#cite_note-ostler-21)

After the [thirteen British colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") revolted and established the United States, [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") and [Henry Knox](/wiki/Henry_Knox "Henry Knox") advocated a "[civilizing](/wiki/Civilizing_mission "Civilizing mission")" policy to assimilate Native Americans as U.S. citizens.[[22]](#cite_note-perdue-22)[[23]](#cite_note-remini_submit_adoption-23)[[24]](#cite_note-eric_miller-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Tom_Jewett-25) Assimilation, whether voluntary—as with the [Choctaw](/wiki/Choctaw "Choctaw")[[26]](#cite_note-us_congress2-26)[[27]](#cite_note-us_citizenship-27)—or [forced](/wiki/Forced_assimilation "Forced assimilation"), remained a central policy. During the 19th century, the ideology of [manifest destiny](/wiki/Manifest_destiny "Manifest destiny") drove westward expansion, increasing pressure on Native lands. The [Indian Removal Act](/wiki/Indian_Removal_Act "Indian Removal Act") of 1830 authorized mass relocations west of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), resulting in ethnic cleansing and forced marches such as the [Trail of Tears](/wiki/Trail_of_Tears "Trail of Tears").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Discovery-statue_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Discovery-statue_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Discovery-statue_(cropped).JPG)

*[The Discovery of America](/wiki/The_Discovery_of_America_(sculpture) "The Discovery of America (sculpture)")* (1844) sculpture, depicting a triumphant Columbus and a "female savage" (Native woman)

A justification for conquest and subjugation stemmed from [dehumanizing](/wiki/Dehumanizing "Dehumanizing") stereotypes like those in the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), which described Native Americans as "merciless Indian savages".[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) Two sculptures reflecting this view of the Natives were commissioned by the [U.S. government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") and stood outside the [U.S. Capitol](/wiki/U.S._Capitol "U.S. Capitol") for over a century: *[The Rescue](/wiki/The_Rescue_(sculpture) "The Rescue (sculpture)")* (1837) whose sculptor [Horatio Greenough](/wiki/Horatio_Greenough "Horatio Greenough") wrote that it was "to convey the idea of the triumph of the whites over the savage tribes",[[30]](#cite_note-30) and *[The Discovery of America](/wiki/The_Discovery_of_America_(sculpture) "The Discovery of America (sculpture)")* (1844) which depicted a triumphant Columbus and a "female savage" according to the Pennsylvania senator [James Buchanan](/wiki/James_Buchanan "James Buchanan") who proposed the sculpture.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Native resistance persisted in the [American Indian Wars](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars"), including the [Dakota War of 1862](/wiki/Dakota_War_of_1862 "Dakota War of 1862"), [Great Sioux War of 1876](/wiki/Great_Sioux_War_of_1876 "Great Sioux War of 1876"), [Snake War](/wiki/Snake_War "Snake War"), [Colorado War](/wiki/Colorado_War "Colorado War"), and [Texas–Indian Wars](/wiki/Texas%E2%80%93Indian_Wars "Texas–Indian Wars"). One of the last major events was the [Wounded Knee Massacre](/wiki/Wounded_Knee_Massacre "Wounded Knee Massacre") in 1890, where up to 300 Lakota were killed during a [Ghost Dance](/wiki/Ghost_Dance "Ghost Dance") gathering.[[32]](#cite_note-Wounded_Knee-32)

In the 20th century, Native Americans served in significant numbers during World War II, marking a turning point in visibility and involvement. Post-war, activism grew with the [American Indian Movement](/wiki/American_Indian_Movement "American Indian Movement") and others pressing for rights. The [Indian Self-Determination and Education Assistance Act](/wiki/Indian_Self-Determination_and_Education_Assistance_Act "Indian Self-Determination and Education Assistance Act") of 1975 recognized tribal autonomy, leading to Native-run schools and economic initiatives. By the 21st century, Native Americans had achieved greater control over tribal lands and resources, though many communities continue to face the legacies of displacement and economic inequality. Over 70% of Native Americans now live in cities, navigating cultural preservation and ongoing discrimination.

Contemporary Native Americans maintain a unique relationship with the United States, with sovereignty and [treaty rights](/wiki/Treaty_rights "Treaty rights") forming the basis of [federal Indian law](/wiki/Federal_Indian_Policy "Federal Indian Policy") and the trust relationship.[[33]](#cite_note-BIA-33) Since the late 1960s, cultural activism has expanded Indigenous presence in politics, media, education, and literature. Independent newspapers, television (e.g. [First Nations Experience](/wiki/First_Nations_Experience "First Nations Experience")), [Native American studies](/wiki/Native_American_studies "Native American studies") programs, tribal schools, and language revitalization efforts have strengthened cultural identity.[[34]](#cite_note-NATV-34)[[35]](#cite_note-NAP-35)

The terms used to refer to Native Americans [have been controversial](/wiki/Native_American_name_controversy "Native American name controversy"). Usage varies by region and generation, with many older Native Americans preferring "Indian" or "American Indian", while younger generations often choose "Indigenous" or "Aboriginal". The term "Native American" has not traditionally included [Native Hawaiians](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians") or some [Alaska Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives") such as [Aleut](/wiki/Aleut "Aleut"), [Yup'ik](/wiki/Yupik_peoples "Yupik peoples"), or [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"),[[36]](#cite_note-36) while in Canada, [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada"), [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit"), and [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis "Métis") are the common designations.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Proportion of Indigenous Americans (Including Native Hawaiians) in each county of the [fifty states](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States "List of states and territories of the United States"), [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") as of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/American_Indian_and_Alaskan_Native_population_pyramid_in_2020.svg/250px-American_Indian_and_Alaskan_Native_population_pyramid_in_2020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:American_Indian_and_Alaskan_Native_population_pyramid_in_2020.svg)

The American Indian and Alaskan Native (alone/single race) populations as of 2020.

The 2020 census reports the U.S. population at 331.4 million; of this, 3.7 million people, or 1.1 percent, reported American Indian or Alaska Native ancestry alone. In addition, 5.9 million people (1.8 percent), reported American Indian or Alaska Native in combination with one or more other races.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The definition of American Indian or Alaska Native used in the 2010 census was as follows:

> According to Office of Management and Budget, "American Indian or Alaska Native" refers to a person having origins in any of the original peoples of North and South America (including Central America) and who maintains tribal affiliation or community attachment.[[39]](#cite_note-2010_Census_AMAN-39)

Despite generally referring to groups indigenous to the continental US, this demographic as defined by the US Census Bureau includes all [Indigenous people of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas"), including [Mesoamerican](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica") peoples such as the [Maya](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples"), as well as [Canadian](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada") and [South American natives](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_South_America "Indigenous peoples of South America").[[40]](#cite_note-40) In 2022, 634,503 Indigenous people in the United States identified with Central American Indigenous groups, 875,183 identified with the [Indigenous people of Mexico](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Mexico "Indigenous peoples of Mexico"), and 47,518 identified with Canadian [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada").[[41]](#cite_note-41) Of the 3.2 million Americans who identified as American Indian or Alaska Native alone in 2022, around 45% are of [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") ethnicity, with this number growing as increasing numbers of Indigenous people from Latin American countries immigrate to the US and more Latinos self-identify with Indigenous heritage.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Of groups Indigenous to the United States, the largest self-reported tribes are [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee "Cherokee") (1,449,888), [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo "Navajo") (434,910), [Choctaw](/wiki/Choctaw "Choctaw") (295,373), [Blackfeet](/wiki/Blackfeet_Nation "Blackfeet Nation") (288,255), [Sioux](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux") (220,739), and [Apache](/wiki/Apache "Apache") (191,823).[[43]](#cite_note-43) 205,954 respondents specified an [Alaska Native](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives") identity.

[Native Hawaiians](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians") are counted separately from Native Americans by the census, being classified as [Pacific Islanders](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_Americans "Pacific Islander Americans"). According to 2022 estimates, 714,847 Americans reported Native Hawaiian ancestry.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The 2010 census permitted respondents to self-identify as being of one or more races. Self-identification dates from the census of 1960; prior to that the race of the respondent was determined by the opinion of the census taker. The option to select more than one race was introduced in 2000.[[45]](#cite_note-Newest_Indians-45) If American Indian or Alaska Native was selected, the form requested the individual provide the name of the "enrolled or principal tribe".

### Population since 1880

Censuses counted around 346,000 Native Americans in 1880 (including 33,000 in Alaska and 82,000 in Oklahoma, back then known as [Indian Territory](/wiki/Indian_Territory "Indian Territory")), around 274,000 in 1890 (including 25,500 in Alaska and 64,500 in Oklahoma), 362,500 in 1930 and 366,500 in 1940, including those on and off reservations in the 48 states and Alaska. Native American population rebounded sharply from 1950, when they numbered 377,273; it reached 551,669 in 1960, 827,268 in 1970, with an annual growth rate of 5%, four times the national average.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Total spending on Native Americans averaged $38 million a year in the late 1920s, dropping to a low of $23 million in 1933, and returning to $38 million in 1940. The [Office of Indian Affairs](/wiki/Bureau_of_Indian_Affairs "Bureau of Indian Affairs") counted more American Indians than the [Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") until 1930:

### Population distribution

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Americanindiansmapcensusbureau.gif/500px-Americanindiansmapcensusbureau.gif)](/wiki/File:Americanindiansmapcensusbureau.gif)

This [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") map depicts the locations of differing Native American groups, including [Indian reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservations "Indian reservations"), as of 2000; present-day [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") in the [Southwestern United States](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States"), which was once designated as an [Indian Territory](/wiki/Indian_Territory "Indian Territory") before Oklahoma's statehood in 1907, is highlighted in blue.

78% of Native Americans live outside a reservation. Mono-racial individuals are more likely to live on a reservation than multiracial individuals. The [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_people "Navajo people"), with 286,000 mono-racial individuals, is the largest tribe if only non-[multiracial](/wiki/Multiracial_people "Multiracial people") individuals are counted; the Navajo are the tribe with the highest proportion of mono-racial individuals, 86.3%. The [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee "Cherokee") have a different history; it is the largest tribe, with 819,000 individuals multiracial, and it has 284,000 mono-racial individuals.[[55]](#cite_note-Navajo2010-57)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

#### Urban migration

Further information: [Urban Indian](/wiki/Urban_Indian "Urban Indian")

As of 2012, 70% of Native Americans live in urban areas, up from 45% in 1970 and 8% in 1940. Urban areas with significant Native American populations include Minneapolis, Denver, Phoenix, Tucson, Chicago, Oklahoma City, Houston, New York City, and Los Angeles. Many live in poverty. Racism, unemployment, drugs and gangs are common problems which Indian social service organizations such as the Little Earth housing complex in Minneapolis attempt to address.[[56]](#cite_note-NYT41413-58)

## Tribal sovereignty

Main articles: [Tribal sovereignty in the United States](/wiki/Tribal_sovereignty_in_the_United_States "Tribal sovereignty in the United States"), [Native American tribe](/wiki/Native_American_tribe "Native American tribe"), and [Indian reservation](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Indian_Reservations.png/250px-Indian_Reservations.png)](/wiki/File:Indian_Reservations.png)

[Indian reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation")

There are 574 [federally recognized tribal governments](/wiki/List_of_Native_American_Tribal_Entities "List of Native American Tribal Entities")[[60]](#cite_note-62) and 326 [Indian reservations](/wiki/List_of_Indian_reservations_in_the_United_States "List of Indian reservations in the United States")[[61]](#cite_note-Department_of_the_Interior-63) in the US. These tribes possess the right to form their own governments, enforce laws (civil and criminal) within their lands, tax, establish requirements for membership, license and regulate activities, zone, and exclude persons from tribal territories. Limitations on self-government include the same applicable to states; for example, neither have the power to make war, engage in foreign relations, or mint currency.[[62]](#cite_note-64) Some tribes are [recognized by individual states](/wiki/List_of_State_Recognized_American_Indian_Tribal_Entities "List of State Recognized American Indian Tribal Entities"), but not by the federal government. The rights and benefits associated with [state recognition](/wiki/Diplomatic_recognition "Diplomatic recognition") vary from state to state.

Many Native Americans and advocates their rights note the federal government's claim to recognize the "sovereignty" of Native American peoples falls short, given the US wishes to govern Native American peoples and treat them as subject to US law.[[63]](#cite_note-65) Such advocates contend that full respect for Native American sovereignty would require the US government to deal with Native American peoples in the same manner as other sovereign nations, handling matters related to Native Americans through the Secretary of State, rather than the [Bureau of Indian Affairs](/wiki/Bureau_of_Indian_Affairs "Bureau of Indian Affairs"). The Bureau of Indian Affairs states that its "responsibility is the administration and management of 55,700,000 acres (225,000 km2) of land held in trust by the United States for American Indians, Indian tribes, and [Alaska Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives")".[[64]](#cite_note-66) Many Native Americans and advocates of their rights believe it is condescending for such lands to be considered "held in trust" and regulated in any fashion by any entity other than the tribes.

Some tribes have been unable to document the continuity required for federal recognition, and its benefits. Tribes must prove continuous existence since 1900. The federal government has maintained this requirement, in part because through participation on councils and committees, federally-recognized tribes have been adamant about groups' satisfying the same requirements as they did.[[65]](#cite_note-home.hamptonroads.com-67) The [Muwekma Ohlone](/wiki/Ohlone "Ohlone") of the San Francisco Bay Area are pursuing litigation in the federal court system to establish recognition.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][[66]](#cite_note-68) Many smaller eastern tribes, long considered remnants of extinct peoples, have been trying to gain recognition of their tribal status. Several tribes in Virginia and North Carolina have gained state recognition. Federal recognition confers benefits, including the right to label arts and crafts as Native American and permission to apply for grants specifically reserved for Native Americans. But gaining recognition as a tribe is difficult; to be established as a tribal group, members have to submit extensive [genealogical](/wiki/Genealogy "Genealogy") proof of tribal descent and continuity of the tribe as a culture.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Abandoned_Mines_Shiprock_2009.jpg/250px-Abandoned_Mines_Shiprock_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abandoned_Mines_Shiprock_2009.jpg)

Native peoples are concerned about the effects of [abandoned uranium mines](/wiki/Uranium_mining_and_the_Navajo_people "Uranium mining and the Navajo people") on or near their lands.

In 2000, the [Washington State Republican Party](/wiki/Washington_State_Republican_Party "Washington State Republican Party") adopted a resolution recommending federal and legislative branches of the [U.S. government](/wiki/U.S._government "U.S. government") terminate tribal governments.[[67]](#cite_note-69) In 2007, a group of [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") congressmen and congresswomen introduced a bill in the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") to terminate federal recognition of the [Cherokee Nation](/wiki/Cherokee_Nation "Cherokee Nation").[[68]](#cite_note-70) This was related to their voting to exclude Cherokee Freedmen as members of the tribe unless they had a Cherokee ancestor on the Dawes Rolls, although all Cherokee Freedmen and their descendants had been members since 1866.

The State of [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine") is the only State House Legislature that allows Representatives from Indian Tribes. The three nonvoting members represent the Penobscot Nation, Houlton Band of Maliseet Indians, and [Passamaquoddy Tribe](/wiki/Passamaquoddy_Tribe "Passamaquoddy Tribe"). These representatives can sponsor any legislation regarding American Indian affairs or co-sponsor pending legislation. Maine is unique regarding Indigenous leadership representation.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

In the state of [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), Native Americans face a unique problem. Until 2017 Virginia had no federally recognized tribes but the state had recognized eight. This is related historically to the greater impact of disease and warfare on the Virginia Indian populations, as well as intermarriage with Europeans and Africans. Some confused ancestry with culture, but groups of Virginia Indians maintained their cultural continuity. Most of their early reservations were ended under the pressure of early European settlement. Historians note the problems of Virginia Indians in establishing continuity of identity, were due to [Walter Ashby Plecker](/wiki/Walter_Ashby_Plecker "Walter Ashby Plecker") (1912–46). As registrar of the state's Bureau of Vital Statistics, he applied his interpretation of the [one-drop rule](/wiki/One-drop_rule "One-drop rule"), enacted in 1924, as the state's Racial Integrity Act. It recognized only two races: "white" and "colored". Plecker, a [segregationist](/wiki/Racial_segregation_in_the_United_States "Racial segregation in the United States"), believed the state's Native Americans had been "mongrelized" by intermarriage with African Americans; to him, ancestry determined identity, rather than culture. He thought some of partial black ancestry were trying to "[pass](/wiki/Passing_(racial_identity) "Passing (racial identity)")" as Native Americans. Plecker thought anyone with African heritage had to be classified as colored. Plecker pressured local governments into reclassifying all Native Americans as "colored" and gave them lists of surnames to examine for reclassification based on his interpretations. This led to the state's destruction of accurate records related to families and communities who identified as Native American. By his actions, sometimes members of the same family were split by being classified as "white" or "colored". He did not allow people to enter their primary identification as Native American in state records.[[65]](#cite_note-home.hamptonroads.com-67) In 2009, the [Senate Indian Affairs Committee](/wiki/Senate_Indian_Affairs_Committee "Senate Indian Affairs Committee") endorsed a bill that would grant federal recognition to tribes in Virginia.[[70]](#cite_note-72)

As of 2000[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Native_Americans_in_the_United_States&action=edit), the largest groups in the US by population were [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_Nation "Navajo Nation"), [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee "Cherokee"), [Choctaw](/wiki/Choctaw "Choctaw"), [Sioux](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux"), [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe"), [Apache](/wiki/Apache "Apache"), [Blackfeet](/wiki/Piegan_Blackfeet "Piegan Blackfeet"), [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), and [Pueblo](/wiki/Puebloan_peoples "Puebloan peoples"). In 2000, eight of ten Americans with Native American ancestry were of mixed ancestry. It is estimated that by 2100 that figure will rise to nine out of ten.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

Native Americans are wary of attempts by others to gain control of their reservation lands for natural resources, such as [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") and [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium") in the West.[[72]](#cite_note-74)[[73]](#cite_note-75)

## Civil rights movement

Main articles: [Civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement"), [Jim Crow Laws](/wiki/Jim_Crow_Laws "Jim Crow Laws"), [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr."), [National Congress of American Indians](/wiki/National_Congress_of_American_Indians "National Congress of American Indians"), [National Indian Youth Council](/wiki/National_Indian_Youth_Council "National Indian Youth Council"), [Native American Rights Fund](/wiki/Native_American_Rights_Fund "Native American Rights Fund"), and [Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education")

[![A group of NIYC demonstrators holding signs in front of the BIA office.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Bia-sit-in.jpg/250px-Bia-sit-in.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bia-sit-in.jpg)

National Indian Youth Council demonstrations, March 1970, Bureau of Indian Affairs Office

The [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement") was significant for the rights of Native Americans and other people of color. Native Americans faced racism and prejudice for hundreds of years, and this increased after the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). Native Americans, like African Americans, were subjected to the [Jim Crow Laws](/wiki/Jim_Crow_Laws "Jim Crow Laws") and segregation in the [Deep South](/wiki/Deep_South "Deep South") especially after they were made citizens through the [Indian Citizenship Act](/wiki/Indian_Citizenship_Act "Indian Citizenship Act") of 1924. As a body of law, Jim Crow institutionalized economic, educational, and social disadvantages for Native Americans, and other people of color living in the south.[[74]](#cite_note-tperd-76)[[75]](#cite_note-jimlu-77)[[76]](#cite_note-jewolf-78) Native American identity was especially targeted by a system that only wanted to recognize white or colored, and the government began to question the legitimacy of some tribes because they had intermarried with African Americans.[[74]](#cite_note-tperd-76)[[75]](#cite_note-jimlu-77) Native Americans were also discriminated and discouraged from voting in the southern and western states.[[76]](#cite_note-jewolf-78)

In the [south](/wiki/Deep_South "Deep South") segregation was a major problem for Native Americans seeking education, but the NAACP's legal strategy would later change this.[[77]](#cite_note-naalega-79) Movements such as [Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education") was a major victory for the Civil Rights Movement headed by the [NAACP](/wiki/NAACP "NAACP"), and inspired Native Americans to start participating in the Civil Rights Movement.[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-kingcreek-81) [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") began assisting Native Americans in the south in the late 1950s after they reached out to him.[[79]](#cite_note-kingcreek-81) At that time the remaining [Creek](/wiki/Creek_tribe "Creek tribe") in Alabama were trying to completely desegregate schools in their area. In this case, light-complexioned Native children were allowed to ride school buses to previously all white schools, while dark-skinned Native children from the same band were barred from riding the same buses.[[79]](#cite_note-kingcreek-81) Tribal leaders, upon hearing of King's desegregation campaign in Birmingham, Alabama, contacted him for assistance. Through King's intervention, the problem was quickly resolved.[[79]](#cite_note-kingcreek-81) King later traveled to Arizona visiting Native Americans on reservations, and in churches encouraging them to be involved in the Civil Rights Movement.[[80]](#cite_note-kingindrez-82) In King's book *Why We Can't Wait* he writes:

> Our nation was born in genocide when it embraced the doctrine that the original American, the Indian, was an inferior race. Even before there were large numbers of Negroes on our shores, the scar of racial hatred had already disfigured colonial society. From the sixteenth century forward, blood flowed in battles over racial supremacy. We are perhaps the only nation which tried as a matter of national policy to wipe out its Indigenous population. Moreover, we elevated that tragic experience into a noble crusade. Indeed, even today we have not permitted ourselves to reject or to feel remorse for this shameful episode. Our literature, our films, our drama, our folklore all exalt it.[[81]](#cite_note-kingnatspeech-83)

Native Americans then actively participated and supported the NAACP, and the civil rights movement.[[82]](#cite_note-hufponat-84) The National Indian Youth Council (NIYC) would soon rise in 1961 to fight for Native American rights during the Civil Rights Movement, and were strong King supporters.[[83]](#cite_note-scielo.org.za-85)[[84]](#cite_note-COBB-86) During the [1963 March on Washington](/wiki/March_on_Washington_for_Jobs_and_Freedom "March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom") there was a sizable Native American contingent, including many from South Dakota, and many from the [Navajo nation](/wiki/Navajo_nation "Navajo nation").[[79]](#cite_note-kingcreek-81)[[85]](#cite_note-navtimes-87) Native Americans also participated the [Poor People's Campaign](/wiki/Poor_People%27s_Campaign "Poor People's Campaign") in 1968.[[83]](#cite_note-scielo.org.za-85) The NIYC were very active supporters of the [Poor People's Campaign](/wiki/Poor_People%27s_Campaign "Poor People's Campaign") unlike the [National Congress of American Indians](/wiki/National_Congress_of_American_Indians "National Congress of American Indians") (NCAI); the NIYC and other Native organizations met with King in March 1968 but the NCAI disagreed on how to approach the anti-poverty campaign; the NCAI decided against participating in the march.[[84]](#cite_note-COBB-86) The NCAI wished to pursue their battles in the courts and with Congress, unlike the NIYC.[[83]](#cite_note-scielo.org.za-85)[[84]](#cite_note-COBB-86) The NAACP also inspired the creation of the [Native American Rights Fund](/wiki/Native_American_Rights_Fund "Native American Rights Fund") (NARF) which was patterned after the NAACP's Legal Defense and Education Fund.[[79]](#cite_note-kingcreek-81) Furthermore, the NAACP continued to organize to stop mass incarceration and end the [criminalization of Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_and_the_prison%E2%80%93industrial_complex "Native Americans and the prison–industrial complex") and other communities of people of color.[[86]](#cite_note-naanative-88) The following is an excerpt from a statement from [Mel Thom](/wiki/Mel_Thom "Mel Thom") on May 1, 1968, during a meeting with Secretary of State [Dean Rusk](/wiki/Dean_Rusk "Dean Rusk"):[[84]](#cite_note-COBB-86) (It was written by members of the Workshop on American Indian Affairs and the NIYC)

> We have joined the Poor People's Campaign because most of our families, tribes, and communities number among those suffering most in this country. We are not begging. We are demanding what is rightfully ours. This is no more than the right to have a decent life in our own communities. We need guaranteed jobs, guaranteed income, housing, schools, economic development, but most important- we want them on our own terms.
> Our chief spokesman in the federal government, the [Department of Interior](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_the_Interior "U.S. Department of the Interior"), has failed us. In fact it began failing us from its very beginning. The Interior Department began failing us because it was built upon and operates under a racist, immoral, paternalistic and colonialistic system. There is no way to improve upon racism, immorality and colonialism; it can only be done away with. The system and power structure serving Indian peoples is a sickness which has grown to epidemic proportions. The Indian system is sick. Paternalism is the virus and the secretary of the Interior is the carrier.

## Contemporary issues

Main article: [Contemporary Native American issues in the United States](/wiki/Contemporary_Native_American_issues_in_the_United_States "Contemporary Native American issues in the United States")

Native American struggles amid [reservation poverty](/wiki/Reservation_poverty "Reservation poverty") to maintain life on the reservation, or in larger society, have resulted in health issues, some related to nutrition and health practices. The community suffers a [vulnerability to, and disproportionately high rate of, alcoholism](/wiki/Alcohol_and_Native_Americans "Alcohol and Native Americans"):[[87]](#cite_note-89)

> It has long been recognized that Native Americans are dying of [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes"), alcoholism, [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"), [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide"), and other health conditions at shocking rates. Beyond disturbingly high mortality rates, Native Americans also suffer a significantly lower health status and disproportionate rates of disease compared with all other Americans.

— [U.S. Commission on Civil Rights](/wiki/United_States_Commission_on_Civil_Rights "United States Commission on Civil Rights")[[88]](#cite_note-90) (September 2004)

Studies point to rising rates of stroke,[[89]](#cite_note-91) heart disease,[[90]](#cite_note-92) and diabetes[[91]](#cite_note-93) in the Native American population.

### Societal discrimination and racism

Further information: [Stereotypes of Native Americans](/wiki/Stereotypes_of_Native_Americans "Stereotypes of Native Americans") and [Racism against Native Americans in the United States](/wiki/Racism_against_Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Racism against Native Americans in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/No_beer_sold_to_indians.jpg/250px-No_beer_sold_to_indians.jpg)](/wiki/File:No_beer_sold_to_indians.jpg)

A [discriminatory](/wiki/Discrimination "Discrimination") sign ("no beer sold to Indians") posted above a bar. [Birney, Montana](/wiki/Birney,_Montana "Birney, Montana"), 1941

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Eight_Crow_prisoners_under_guard_at_Crow_agency%2C_Montana%2C_1887_-_NARA_-_531126.jpg/250px-Eight_Crow_prisoners_under_guard_at_Crow_agency%2C_Montana%2C_1887_-_NARA_-_531126.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eight_Crow_prisoners_under_guard_at_Crow_agency,_Montana,_1887_-_NARA_-_531126.jpg)

Chief Plenty Coups and eight Crow prisoners under guard at Crow agency, Montana, 1887

Native Americans have been subjected to discrimination for centuries. In response to being labeled "merciless Indian savages" in the Declaration of Independence, Simon Moya-Smith states, "Any holiday that would refer to my people in such a repugnant, racist manner is certainly not worth celebrating. [July Fourth] is a day we celebrate our resiliency, our culture, our languages, our children and we mourn the millions — literally millions — of Indigenous people who have died as a consequence of American imperialism."[[92]](#cite_note-94)

In a study conducted in 2006–07, non-Native Americans stated they rarely encountered Native Americans. This is largely due to the number of Native Americans having dwindled since white [settler colonialism](/wiki/Settler_colonialism "Settler colonialism"), while those who survived were forcibly moved into reservations. Both factors were referenced by [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") in 1928 when he admiringly stated the US had "gunned down the millions of Redskins to a few hundred thousand, and now keep the modest remnant under observation in a cage".[[93]](#cite_note-95)[[94]](#cite_note-96) While sympathetic toward Native Americans and expressing regret over the past, most had only a vague understanding of the problems facing Native Americans. Native Americans told researchers they believed they continued to face [prejudice](/wiki/Racism_in_the_United_States "Racism in the United States"), mistreatment, and [inequality](/wiki/Native_Americans_and_reservation_inequality "Native Americans and reservation inequality") in society.[[95]](#cite_note-97)

#### Affirmative action issues

Federal contractors and subcontractors, such as businesses and educational institutions, are legally required to adopt [equal opportunity employment](/wiki/Equal_opportunity_employment "Equal opportunity employment") and [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action_in_the_United_States "Affirmative action in the United States") measures intended to prevent discrimination against employees or applicants for employment on the basis of "color, religion, sex, or national origin".[[96]](#cite_note-98)[[97]](#cite_note-ref1-99) For this purpose, a Native American is defined as "A person having origins in any of the original peoples of North and South America (including Central America), and who maintains a tribal affiliation or community attachment". The passing of the [Indian Relocation Act of 1956](/wiki/Indian_Relocation_Act_of_1956 "Indian Relocation Act of 1956") saw a 56% increase in Native American city dwellers over 40 years.[[98]](#cite_note-AmericanIndianPoverty-100) The Native American urban poverty rate exceeds that of reservation poverty rates due to discrimination in hiring processes.[[98]](#cite_note-AmericanIndianPoverty-100) However, self-reporting is permitted: "Educational institutions and other recipients should allow students and staff to self-identify their race and ethnicity unless self-identification is not practicable or feasible."[[99]](#cite_note-FRDefinition-101) Self-reporting opened the door to "box checking" and self-identification by people who, despite not having a substantial relationship to Native American culture, innocently or fraudulently check the box for Native American.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

The difficulties Native Americans face in the workforce, including lack of promotions and wrongful terminations are attributed to racial stereotypes and implicit biases. Native American business owners are seldom offered auxiliary resources crucial for entrepreneurial success.[[98]](#cite_note-AmericanIndianPoverty-100)

### Sexual violence as a tool for settler colonialism

Throughout history, settler colonialism has remained a violent tool to displace and exterminate Native American peoples. The use of sexual violence to perpetuate this is common. Professor Sarah Deer highlights the high number of Native women who still experience this violence: "Since 1999 a variety of reports and studies have come to the same conclusion- namely, that Native women in particular suffer the highest rate of per capita rape in the US." The continued acts of sexual violence against Native women have been perpetuated by colonization and the actions of colonizers. Native women through time have been portrayed as extremely sexual which only enforces sexual violence. Deer explains, "Dispossession and relocation of indigenous peoples on this continent both necessitated and precipitated a highly gendered and sexualized dynamic in which Native women's bodies became commodities- bought and sold for the purposes of sexual gratification (or profit), invariably transporting them far away from their homes."[[101]](#cite_note-103)

### Native American mascots

Main article: [Native American mascot controversy](/wiki/Native_American_mascot_controversy "Native American mascot controversy")

Further information: [NCAA Native American mascot decision](/wiki/NCAA_Native_American_mascot_decision "NCAA Native American mascot decision")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/NotYourMascot2.jpg/250px-NotYourMascot2.jpg)](/wiki/File:NotYourMascot2.jpg)

Protest against the name of the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins") in Minneapolis, November 2014

American Indian activists in the US and Canada have criticized the use of Native American [mascots](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot") in sports, as perpetuating stereotypes. This is considered [cultural appropriation](/wiki/Cultural_appropriation "Cultural appropriation"). There has been a decline in the number of secondary school and college teams using such names, images, and mascots. Some tribal team names have been approved by the tribe in question, such as the Seminole Council of Florida approving use of their name for the teams of [Florida State University](/wiki/Florida_State_University "Florida State University").[[102]](#cite_note-104)[[103]](#cite_note-teachingTolerance-105) The NCAA allows the use even though the NCAA "continues to believe the stereotyping of Native Americans is wrong."[[104]](#cite_note-106)

Among professional teams, the [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA")'s [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors") discontinued use of Native American-themed logos in 1971. The [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL")'s [Washington Commanders](/wiki/Washington_Commanders "Washington Commanders"), formerly the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins_name_controversy "Washington Redskins name controversy"), changed their name in 2020, as [the term](/wiki/Redskin "Redskin") is considered a racial slur.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

[MLB](/wiki/MLB "MLB")'s [Cleveland Guardians](/wiki/Cleveland_Guardians "Cleveland Guardians") were formerly known as the [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians_name_and_logo_controversy "Cleveland Indians name and logo controversy"). Their use of a caricature called [Chief Wahoo](/wiki/Chief_Wahoo "Chief Wahoo") faced protest for decades.[[106]](#cite_note-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109) Starting in 2019, Chief Wahoo ceased to be a logo for Cleveland Indians.[[108]](#cite_note-110)[[109]](#cite_note-111)[[110]](#cite_note-WahooLogoNYT-112)[[111]](#cite_note-WahooLogoNBC-113) In December 2020, *The New York Times* reported that Cleveland would be officially changing their name,[[112]](#cite_note-114) and in 2021, became the [Cleveland Guardians](/wiki/Cleveland_Guardians "Cleveland Guardians").[[113]](#cite_note-ClevelandGuardians-115)[[114]](#cite_note-116)

### Historical depictions in art

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/North_carolina_algonkin-rituale02.jpg/250px-North_carolina_algonkin-rituale02.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_carolina_algonkin-rituale02.jpg)

*[Secotan](/wiki/Secotan "Secotan") Indians' dance in North Carolina*. Watercolor by John White, 1585.

Native Americans have been depicted by [American artists](/wiki/List_of_American_artists "List of American artists") in various ways at different periods. Several 19th- and 20th-century US and Canadian painters, often motivated by a desire to document and preserve Native culture, specialized in Native American subjects. Among the most prominent were [Elbridge Ayer Burbank](/wiki/Elbridge_Ayer_Burbank "Elbridge Ayer Burbank"), [George Catlin](/wiki/George_Catlin "George Catlin"), [Seth Eastman](/wiki/Seth_Eastman "Seth Eastman"), [Paul Kane](/wiki/Paul_Kane "Paul Kane"), [W. Langdon Kihn](/wiki/W._Langdon_Kihn "W. Langdon Kihn"), [Charles Bird King](/wiki/Charles_Bird_King "Charles Bird King"), [Joseph Henry Sharp](/wiki/Joseph_Henry_Sharp "Joseph Henry Sharp"), and [John Mix Stanley](/wiki/John_Mix_Stanley "John Mix Stanley").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/George_Catlin_-_Eagle_Dance_-_1985.66.386%2C440_-_Smithsonian_American_Art_Museum.jpg/250px-George_Catlin_-_Eagle_Dance_-_1985.66.386%2C440_-_Smithsonian_American_Art_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Catlin_-_Eagle_Dance_-_1985.66.386,440_-_Smithsonian_American_Art_Museum.jpg)

Eagle Dance of the [Sac and Fox](/wiki/Sac_and_Fox_Nation "Sac and Fox Nation") Indians, painting by [George Catlin](/wiki/George_Catlin "George Catlin"), c. 1845

In the 20th century, early portrayals of Native Americans in [movies](/wiki/Movies "Movies") and [television](/wiki/Television "Television") roles were first performed by European Americans dressed in mock traditional attire. Examples included *The Last of the Mohicans* (1920), *Hawkeye and the Last of the Mohicans* (1957), and *[F Troop](/wiki/F_Troop "F Troop")* (1965–67). In later decades, Native American actors such as [Jay Silverheels](/wiki/Jay_Silverheels "Jay Silverheels") in *[The Lone Ranger](/wiki/The_Lone_Ranger#Television_series "The Lone Ranger")* television series (1949–57) came to prominence. The roles of Native Americans were limited and not reflective of Native American culture. By the 1970s some Native American roles began to show more complexity, such as those in *[Little Big Man](/wiki/Little_Big_Man_(film) "Little Big Man (film)")* (1970), *[Billy Jack](/wiki/Billy_Jack "Billy Jack")* (1971), and *[The Outlaw Josey Wales](/wiki/The_Outlaw_Josey_Wales "The Outlaw Josey Wales")* (1976), which depicted Native Americans in supporting roles.

For years, Native people on American television were relegated to secondary, subordinate roles. In *[Bonanza](/wiki/Bonanza "Bonanza")* (1959–73), no major or secondary Native characters appeared on a consistent basis. The series *[The Lone Ranger](/wiki/The_Lone_Ranger_(TV_series) "The Lone Ranger (TV series)")* (1949–57), *[Cheyenne](/wiki/Cheyenne_(1955_TV_series) "Cheyenne (1955 TV series)")* (1955–63), and *[Law of the Plainsman](/wiki/Law_of_the_Plainsman "Law of the Plainsman")* (1959–63) had Native characters who were aides to the central white characters. This continued in such series as *[How the West Was Won](/wiki/How_the_West_Was_Won_(TV_series) "How the West Was Won (TV series)")*. These programs resembled the "sympathetic" yet contradictory film *[Dances With Wolves](/wiki/Dances_With_Wolves "Dances With Wolves")* of 1990, in which, the narrative choice was to relate the Lakota story through a Euro-American voice, for wider impact among a general audience.[[115]](#cite_note-117)
Like the 1992 remake of *[The Last of the Mohicans](/wiki/The_Last_of_the_Mohicans_(1992_film) "The Last of the Mohicans (1992 film)")* and *[Geronimo: An American Legend](/wiki/Geronimo:_An_American_Legend "Geronimo: An American Legend")* (1993), *Dances with Wolves* employed Native American actors, and made an effort to portray Indigenous languages. In 1996, [Plains Cree](/wiki/Plains_Cree_people "Plains Cree people") actor [Michael Greyeyes](/wiki/Michael_Greyeyes "Michael Greyeyes") would play renowned Native American warrior [Crazy Horse](/wiki/Crazy_Horse "Crazy Horse") in the 1996 television film *[Crazy Horse](/wiki/Crazy_Horse_(1996_film) "Crazy Horse (1996 film)")*,[[116]](#cite_note-118) and renowned Sioux chief [Sitting Bull](/wiki/Sitting_Bull "Sitting Bull") in the 2017 movie *[Woman Walks Ahead](/wiki/Woman_Walks_Ahead "Woman Walks Ahead")*.[[117]](#cite_note-119)

The 1998 film *[Smoke Signals](/wiki/Smoke_Signals_(film) "Smoke Signals (film)")*, set on the [Coeur D'Alene Reservation](/wiki/Coeur_D%27Alene_Reservation "Coeur D'Alene Reservation"), discussed hardships of present-day American Indian families, featured numerous Native American actors.[[118]](#cite_note-hollywoodmilestone-120) It was the first feature film to be produced and directed by Native Americans, and to have an exclusive Native American cast.[[118]](#cite_note-hollywoodmilestone-120) At the Sundance Film Festival, *Smoke Signals* won the Audience Award and its producer [Chris Eyre](/wiki/Chris_Eyre "Chris Eyre"), an enrolled member of the Cheyenne and Arapaho Tribes of Oklahoma, won the Filmmaker's Trophy.[[119]](#cite_note-121) In 2009, *[We Shall Remain](/wiki/We_Shall_Remain_(documentary) "We Shall Remain (documentary)")*, a documentary by [Ric Burns](/wiki/Ric_Burns "Ric Burns") and part of the *[American Experience](/wiki/American_Experience "American Experience")* series, presented a series "from a Native American perspective". It represented "an unprecedented collaboration between Native and non-Native filmmakers and involves Native advisors and scholars at all levels of the project".[[120]](#cite_note-122) The episodes explore the impact of [King Philip's War](/wiki/King_Philip%27s_War "King Philip's War") on the northeastern tribes, the "Native American confederacy" of [Tecumseh's War](/wiki/Tecumseh%27s_War "Tecumseh's War"), U.S.-forced relocation of Southeastern tribes, the pursuit and capture of [Geronimo](/wiki/Geronimo "Geronimo") and [Apache Wars](/wiki/Apache_Wars "Apache Wars"), and concludes with the [Wounded Knee incident](/wiki/Wounded_Knee_incident "Wounded Knee incident"), participation by the [American Indian Movement](/wiki/American_Indian_Movement "American Indian Movement"), and the increasing resurgence of modern Native cultures since.

### Differences in terminology

Further information: [Native American name controversy](/wiki/Native_American_name_controversy "Native American name controversy")

The most common of modern terms to refer to Indigenous peoples of the United States are *Indians*, *American Indians*, and *Native Americans*. Up to the early to mid 18th century, the term *Americans* was not applied to people of European heritage in North America. Instead it was equivalent to the term *Indians*. As people of European heritage began using the term *Americans* to refer instead to themselves, the word *Indians* became historically the most often employed term.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

The term *Indians*, long laden with racist stereotypes, began to be replaced in the 1960s with the term *Native Americans*, which recognized the Indigeneity of the people who first made the Americas home. But as the term *Native Americans* became popular, the American Indian Movement saw pejorative connotations in the term *native* and reappropriated the term *Indian*, seeing it as witness to the history of violence against the many nations that lived in the Americas before European arrival.[[122]](#cite_note-124)

The term *Native American* was introduced in the US in preference to the older term *Indian* to distinguish the [Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") from the people of [India](/wiki/India "India"). It may have been coined by [Mohican](/wiki/Mohican "Mohican") Sachem [John Wannuaucon Quinney](/wiki/John_Wannuaucon_Quinney "John Wannuaucon Quinney"), in an 1852 address to the US Congress where he argued against proposed resettlement.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

The term *Amerindian*, a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of "American Indian", was coined in 1902 by the [American Anthropological Association](/wiki/American_Anthropological_Association "American Anthropological Association"). However, it has been controversial since its creation. It was immediately rejected by some leading members of the Association, and, while adopted by many, it was never universally accepted.[[124]](#cite_note-AmerIndNYT-126) While never popular in Indigenous communities themselves, it remains a preferred term among some anthropologists.[[125]](#cite_note-127)[[126]](#cite_note-128)[[127]](#cite_note-129)[[128]](#cite_note-130)

During World War II, draft boards typically classified American Indians from Virginia as [Negroes](/wiki/Negro "Negro").[[129]](#cite_note-131)[[130]](#cite_note-132)

In 1995, a plurality of Indigenous Americans, preferred the term *American Indian*[[131]](#cite_note-Census-133) and many [tribes](/wiki/List_of_federally_recognized_tribes "List of federally recognized tribes") include the word Indian in their formal title.

Criticism of the [neologism](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") *Native American* comes from diverse sources. [Russell Means](/wiki/Russell_Means "Russell Means"), an Oglala Lakota activist, opposed the term *Native American* because he believed it was imposed by the government without the consent of Native people.[[132]](#cite_note-134)

A 1995 U.S. Census Bureau survey found that more Native Americans in the United States preferred *American Indian* to *Native American*.[[131]](#cite_note-Census-133) Most American Indians are comfortable with *Indian*, *American Indian*, and *Native American*.[[133]](#cite_note-135) That term is reflected in the name chosen for the [National Museum of the American Indian](/wiki/National_Museum_of_the_American_Indian "National Museum of the American Indian"), which opened in 2004 on [the Mall](/wiki/The_Mall_(Washington,_D.C.) "The Mall (Washington, D.C.)") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.").

Other commonly used terms are *First Americans*, *First Nations*, and *Native Peoples*.[[134]](#cite_note-136)

### Colonial ecological violence

Colonial ecological violence, defined by sociologist J. M. Bacon as the result of eco-social disruptions that "generate colonial ecological violence, a unique form of violence perpetrated by the settler-colonial state, private industry, and settler-colonial culture as a whole."[[135]](#cite_note-137) The relocation and displacement of Native peoples is a result of the colonizer mindset that land is a commodity. By removing these communities from their land, settlers are preventing the ways of life and use of culture-affirming resources. Gilio-Whitaker highlights some of the ways in which these practices are reinforced, with the concept of environmental deprivation – "historical processes of land and resource dispossession calculated to bring about the destruction of Indigenous lives and cultures." The reason these lands are so important to Native populations is because, "Since a strong component of many Indigenous cultures is a robust relationship to place, it serves to reason that forced removals, settler resource appropriation, and the ecological damage perpetuated by US settle colonial society contribute to significant "conflict" between "traditional cultural values" and "those of majority culture".[[136]](#cite_note-138)

The Karuk tribe in Klamath, California are one of many victims of colonial ecological violence. A major way of life is the use of fires to maintain and regulate their environment. These fires were used to correct travel routes and optimize hunting, a major part of Karuk life. In 1905, the Klamath National Forest was established which prevented the burning of fires on Karuk land- "Fire exclusion, then, has simultaneously produced Indigenous exclusion, erasure, and replacement." This land is one of the most economically wealthy spots due to the establishment of the forest, which further demonstrates the ways in which settler-colonialism enables and continues to negatively impact the land that Indigenous people lived on.[[137]](#cite_note-139)

The Potawatomi tribe had long occupied the Great Lakes region, up until they were displaced and spread out around the US. They had previously lived on 30 million acres of land, building cultural, familial, and other-than-human relationships for generations.[[138]](#cite_note-Whyte-140) Kyle Powys Whyte highlights the ways in which this displacement has had violent and detrimental impacts on the tribe. "The consequences of capitalist economics, such as deforestation, water pollution, the clearing of land for large scale agriculture and urbanization, generate immediate disruptions on ecosystems "rapidly" rendering them very different from what they were like before, undermining Indigenous knowledge systems and Indigenous peoples' capacity to cultivate landscapes and adjust to environmental change."[[138]](#cite_note-Whyte-140)

The [Miami Tribe of Oklahoma](/wiki/Miami_Tribe_of_Oklahoma "Miami Tribe of Oklahoma"), once resided in Oxford, Ohio, where Miami University now is placed. In 1818, the tribe agreed to give up a large amount of land to U.S. officials. It was not until 1826 that [Lewis Cass](/wiki/Lewis_Cass "Lewis Cass") informed them and nearby Potawatomi, "You must remove or perish."[[139]](#cite_note-:0-141) This plan did not work, but the officials persisted and eventually the Miami tribe were forced off their land in 1846. Miami University has a land acknowledgement document and a center dedicated to working with the Miami tribe of Oklahoma, though this is the only tribe from the original Miami tribe that is accredited by the U.S. government.[[140]](#cite_note-142)

### Gambling industry

Main article: [Native American gaming](/wiki/Native_American_gaming "Native American gaming")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Sandia_Casino%2C_detail.jpg/250px-Sandia_Casino%2C_detail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sandia_Casino,_detail.jpg)

Sandia Casino, owned by the [Sandia Pueblo](/wiki/Sandia_Pueblo "Sandia Pueblo") of [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico")

Because [Indian reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation") have [tribal sovereignty](/wiki/Tribal_sovereignty "Tribal sovereignty"), states have limited ability to forbid gambling there, as codified by the [Indian Gaming Regulatory Act](/wiki/Indian_Gaming_Regulatory_Act "Indian Gaming Regulatory Act") of 1988. Tribes run [casinos](/wiki/Casino "Casino"), [bingo](/wiki/Bingo_(U.S.) "Bingo (U.S.)") halls, and other [gambling](/wiki/Gambling "Gambling") operations, and as of 2011, there were 460 such operations run by 240 tribes,[[141]](#cite_note-143) with a total annual revenue of $27 billion.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

### Financial services

Numerous tribes have entered financial services, including the [Otoe-Missouria](/wiki/Otoe-Missouria "Otoe-Missouria"), [Tunica-Biloxi](/wiki/Tunica-Biloxi "Tunica-Biloxi"), and the [Rosebud Sioux](/wiki/Rosebud_Indian_Reservation "Rosebud Indian Reservation"). Because of the challenges involved in starting a financial services business, many tribes hire outside consultants and vendors to help them launch these businesses and manage the regulatory issues. Similar to the tribal sovereignty debates that occurred when tribes first entered the gaming industry, the tribes, states, and federal government are in disagreement regarding who possesses the authority to regulate these e-commerce business entities.[[143]](#cite_note-145)

### Crime on reservations

Prosecution of serious crime, historically endemic on reservations,[[144]](#cite_note-DOJStat-146)[[145]](#cite_note-MLRWashburn-147) was required by the 1885 Major Crimes Act,[[146]](#cite_note-DP1885-148) 18 U.S.C. §§1153, 3242, and court decisions to be investigated by the federal government, usually the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation"), and prosecuted by [United States Attorneys](/wiki/United_States_Attorney "United States Attorney") of the [United States federal judicial district](/wiki/United_States_federal_judicial_district "United States federal judicial district") in which the reservation lies.[[147]](#cite_note-DP2010-149)[[148]](#cite_note-DP730-150)[[149]](#cite_note-DP2007-151)[[150]](#cite_note-NYT111212-152)[[151]](#cite_note-153)

A 2009 *[New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* article about growing [gang violence](/wiki/Gangs_in_the_United_States "Gangs in the United States") on the [Pine Ridge Indian Reservation](/wiki/Pine_Ridge_Indian_Reservation "Pine Ridge Indian Reservation") estimated there were 39 gangs with 5,000 members on that reservation alone.[[152]](#cite_note-154) [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_Nation "Navajo Nation") country reported 225 gangs in its territory.[[153]](#cite_note-155)

As of 2012, a high incidence of rape continued to impact Native American and Alaskan native women. According to the Department of Justice, 1 in 3 Native women have suffered rape or attempted rape, more than twice the national rate.[[154]](#cite_note-NYTRape2012-156) About 46 percent of Native American women have been raped, beaten, or stalked by an intimate partner, according to a 2010 study by the [Centers for Disease Control](/wiki/Centers_for_Disease_Control "Centers for Disease Control").[[155]](#cite_note-157) "More than 80 percent of Indian victims identify their attacker as non-Indian".[[156]](#cite_note-NYTDuthu-158)[[157]](#cite_note-NYT021013-159)

### Barriers to economic development

Other than tribes successfully running casinos, many tribes struggle, as they are often located on reservations isolated from economic centers. The estimated 2.1 million Native Americans are the most impoverished of all ethnic groups. According to the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"), an estimated 400,000 Native Americans reside on reservation land. While some tribes have had success with gaming, only 40% of the 562 federally recognized tribes operate casinos.[[158]](#cite_note-NIGA-160) According to a 2007 survey, only 1% of Native Americans own and operate a business.[[159]](#cite_note-SBA-161)

The barriers to [economic development](/wiki/Economic_development "Economic development") on Native American reservations identified by the [Harvard Project on American Indian Economic Development](/wiki/Harvard_Project_on_American_Indian_Economic_Development "Harvard Project on American Indian Economic Development"), in *What Can Tribes Do? Strategies and Institutions in American Indian Economic Development* (2008),[[160]](#cite_note-162) are as follows:

* Lack of access to capital
* Lack of human capital (education, skills, technical expertise) and the means to develop it
* Reservations lack effective planning
* Reservations are poor in natural resources
* Reservations have natural resources but lack sufficient control over them
* Reservations are disadvantaged by their distance from markets and the high costs of transportation
* Tribes cannot persuade investors to locate on reservations because of intense competition from non-Native American communities
* The [Bureau of Indian Affairs](/wiki/Bureau_of_Indian_Affairs "Bureau of Indian Affairs") is inept, corrupt or uninterested in reservation development
* Tribal politicians and bureaucrats are inept or corrupt
* On-reservation factionalism destroys stability in tribal decisions
* The instability of tribal government keeps outsiders from investing. The lack of international recognition Native American tribal sovereignty weakens their political-economic legitimacy.[[161]](#cite_note-NativeAmericanEconomicDevelopment-163)
* Entrepreneurial skills and experience are scarce

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Teacher_with_picture_cards_giving_English_instruction_to_Navajo_day_school_students_-_NARA_-_295158.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Teacher_with_picture_cards_giving_English_instruction_to_Navajo_day_school_students_-_NARA_-_295158.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Teacher_with_picture_cards_giving_English_instruction_to_Navajo_day_school_students_-_NARA_-_295158.tif)

Teacher with picture cards giving English instruction to Navajo day school students

A major barrier is lack of entrepreneurial knowledge and experience within [Indian reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservations "Indian reservations"). "A general lack of education and experience about business is a significant challenge to prospective entrepreneurs", was the report on Native American [entrepreneurship](/wiki/Entrepreneurship "Entrepreneurship") by the Northwest Area Foundation in 2004.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][[162]](#cite_note-CFED-164)

### Discourse in Native American economic development

Some scholars argue existing theories and practices of [economic development](/wiki/Economic_development "Economic development") are not suitable for Native American communities—given the lifestyle, economic, and cultural differences, as well as the history of Native American-U.S. relations.[[161]](#cite_note-NativeAmericanEconomicDevelopment-163) Little economic development research has been conducted on Native American communities. The federal government fails to consider place-based issues of American Indian poverty by generalizing the demographic.[[161]](#cite_note-NativeAmericanEconomicDevelopment-163)[[163]](#cite_note-:2-165) The dominance of federal government involvement in Indigenous developmental activities perpetuates and exacerbates the [salvage paradigm](http://www.panya.ca/publication_salvage_paradigm_introduction.php).[[161]](#cite_note-NativeAmericanEconomicDevelopment-163)

### Land ownership challenges

Native land owned by individual Native Americans sometimes cannot be developed because of fractionalization. Fractionalization occurs when a landowner dies, and their land is inherited by their children, but not subdivided. This means one parcel might be owned by 50 different individuals. A majority of those holding interest must agree to any proposal to develop the land, and establishing this is time-consuming, cumbersome, and sometimes impossible.

Another issue is [checkerboarding](/wiki/Checkerboarding_(land) "Checkerboarding (land)"), where tribal land is interspersed with land owned by the federal government on behalf of Natives, individually-owned plots, and land owned by non-Native individuals. This prevents Tribal governments from securing plots of land large enough for economic development or agricultural uses.[[164]](#cite_note-166) Because reservation land is owned "in trust" by the federal government, individuals living on reservations cannot build equity in their homes. This bars Native Americans from getting loans, as there is nothing that a bank can collect if the loan is not paid. Efforts to encourage land ownership (such as the [Dawes Act](/wiki/Dawes_Act "Dawes Act")) resulted in a net loss of tribal land. After they were familiarized with their [smallholder](/wiki/Smallholding "Smallholding") status, Native American landowners were lifted of trust restrictions and their land would get transferred back to them, contingent on a transactional fee to the federal government. The transfer fee discouraged Native American land ownership, with 65% of tribal-owned land being sold to non-Native Americans by the 1920s.[[165]](#cite_note-:3-167) Activists against property rights point to historical evidence of communal ownership of land and resources by tribes. They claim that because of this history, property rights are foreign to Natives and have no place in the modern reservation system. Those in favor of property rights cite examples of tribes negotiating with colonial communities or other tribes about fishing and hunting rights.[[166]](#cite_note-168) Land ownership was a challenge because of the different definitions of land that the Natives and Europeans had.[[167]](#cite_note-169) Most tribes thought of property rights more as "borrowing" the land, while those from Europe thought of land as individual property.[[168]](#cite_note-170)

State-level efforts such as the [Oklahoma Indian Welfare Act](/wiki/Oklahoma_Indian_Welfare_Act "Oklahoma Indian Welfare Act") were attempts to contain tribal land in Native American hands.
