The knowledge disconnect between the decision-making bureaucracy and Native American stakeholders resulted in ineffective development efforts.[[163]](#cite_note-:2-165)[[165]](#cite_note-:3-167) Traditional Native American entrepreneurship does not prioritize [profit maximization](/wiki/Profit_maximization "Profit maximization"); rather, business transactions must align with Native American social and cultural values.[[169]](#cite_note-171) In response to Indigenous business philosophy, the federal government created policies that aimed to formalize their business practices, which undermined the Native American status quo.[[165]](#cite_note-:3-167) Legal disputes interfered with tribal land leasing, which were settled with the verdict against [tribal sovereignty](/wiki/Tribal_sovereignty_in_the_United_States "Tribal sovereignty in the United States").[[170]](#cite_note-172)

Often, bureaucratic overseers of development are removed from Native American communities and lack the knowledge and understanding to develop plans or make resource allocation decisions.[[163]](#cite_note-:2-165) The top-down heavy involvement in developmental operations, does not mitigate incentives for bureaucrats to act in their self-interest. Such instances include reports that exaggerate results.[[163]](#cite_note-:2-165)

### Trauma

[Historical trauma](/wiki/Historical_trauma "Historical trauma") is described as collective emotional and psychological damage throughout a person's lifetime and across multiple generations.[[171]](#cite_note-Myhra2011-173) Examples can be seen through the [Wounded Knee Massacre](/wiki/Wounded_Knee_Massacre "Wounded Knee Massacre") of 1890, where over 200 unarmed Lakota were killed,[[172]](#cite_note-Weaver-174) and the [Dawes Act](/wiki/Dawes_Act "Dawes Act") of 1887, when Native Americans lost four-fifths of their land.[[173]](#cite_note-Brave-175)

[![An older Native American woman talks behind a table of beans, grains, and other produce. She is demonstrating the different traditional Native American foods.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Native_food_demonstration%2C_table_filled_with_wooden_bowls_of_beans%2C_grains%2C_and_produce_%2823de14d7-eb2b-439c-b06f-9338dcb79f75%29.JPG/250px-Native_food_demonstration%2C_table_filled_with_wooden_bowls_of_beans%2C_grains%2C_and_produce_%2823de14d7-eb2b-439c-b06f-9338dcb79f75%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Native_food_demonstration,_table_filled_with_wooden_bowls_of_beans,_grains,_and_produce_(23de14d7-eb2b-439c-b06f-9338dcb79f75).JPG)

A Native American woman talks behind a table of bowls of beans, grains, and other produce at an Indigenous food demonstration.

Native American youth have higher rates of substance and alcohol use deaths than the general population.[[174]](#cite_note-Paul-176) Many Native Americans can trace the beginning of their substance and alcohol use to a traumatic event related to their offender's own substance use.[[175]](#cite_note-Myhra2014-177) A person's substance use can be described as a defense mechanism against the user's emotions and trauma.[[176]](#cite_note-Cole-178) For Native Americans, alcoholism is a symptom of trauma passed from generation to generation and influenced by oppressive behaviors and policies by the dominant European-American society.[[177]](#cite_note-Coyhis-179) Boarding schools were made to "Kill the Indian, Save the man".[[178]](#cite_note-Gray-180) Shame among Native Americans can be attributed to years of oppression and annihilation.[[176]](#cite_note-Cole-178)

### Food insecurity

Studies are being conducted which show Native Americans often experience higher rates of food insecurity than other racial groups in the US. The studies do not focus on the overall picture of Native American households, however, and tend to focus on smaller sample sizes.[[179]](#cite_note-181) In a study that evaluated food insecurity among Indigenous Americans, White, Black, Hispanic, and Asian: it was reported that over the 10-year span of 2000–10, Indigenous people were reported to be one of the highest at-risk groups from a lack of access to adequate food, reporting about 25% of households suffering from this type of insecurity. There are many reasons, the biggest is high food costs on or near reservations, lack of access to well-paying jobs, and predisposition to health issues relating to obesity and mental health.[[180]](#cite_note-182)

## Society, language, and culture

Main article: [Native American cultures in the United States](/wiki/Native_American_cultures_in_the_United_States "Native American cultures in the United States")

Further information: [Category:Archaeological cultures of North America](/wiki/Category:Archaeological_cultures_of_North_America "Category:Archaeological cultures of North America")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Warm_Springs.jpg/250px-Warm_Springs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Warm_Springs.jpg)

Native American women in Warm Springs Indian Reservation, Wasco County, Oregon (1902)

The culture of Pre-Columbian America is usually defined by the concept of the culture area, namely a region where shared cultural traits occur. The northwest culture area, for example, shared common traits such as salmon fishing, woodworking, and large villages or towns, and a hierarchical social structure.[[181]](#cite_note-183) Ethnographers generally [classify the Indigenous peoples of North America](/wiki/Classification_of_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Classification of Indigenous peoples of the Americas") into ten cultural areas based on region.

Though cultural features, language, clothing, and customs vary from one tribe to another, there are certain elements which are encountered frequently and shared by many tribes. Early scholars described the Native Americans as having a society dominated by [clans](/wiki/Clan "Clan").[[182]](#cite_note-Morgan1907-184)

European colonization had a major impact on Native American cultures through what is known as the [Columbian exchange](/wiki/Columbian_exchange "Columbian exchange"), which was the widespread transfer of plants, animals, culture, human populations, technology, and ideas between the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") and Eurasia (the [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World")) in the 15th and 16th centuries, following [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus")'s [1492 voyage](/wiki/Voyages_of_Christopher_Columbus#First_voyage_(1492–1493) "Voyages of Christopher Columbus").[[183]](#cite_note-history-185) They exchanged food, crafts, and furs for blankets, iron and steel implements, horses, trinkets, firearms, and alcoholic beverages. The Columbian exchange generally had a destructive impact on Native American cultures through disease, and a 'clash of cultures',[[184]](#cite_note-Emmer2003-186) whereby European values of private land ownership, the family, and division of labor, led to conflict, appropriation of traditional communal lands and changed how Indigenous tribes practiced slavery.[[184]](#cite_note-Emmer2003-186)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/GeronimoRinehart.jpg/250px-GeronimoRinehart.jpg)](/wiki/File:GeronimoRinehart.jpg)

[Geronimo](/wiki/Geronimo "Geronimo"), Chiricahua Apache leader, 1898, by [Frank Rinehart](/wiki/Frank_Rinehart "Frank Rinehart")

The impact of the Columbian exchange was not entirely negative. For example, the re-introduction of the horse to North America allowed the Plains Indians to revolutionize their ways of life by making hunting, trading, and warfare more effective, and to improve their ability to transport possessions and move settlements.[[185]](#cite_note-187) The Great Plains tribes were still hunting bison when they first encountered Europeans. Reintroduction of the horse changed the way in which they hunted large game. Horses became such a valuable, central element of Native lives that they were counted as a measure of wealth by many tribes.

### Ethno-linguistic classification

Main article: [Indigenous languages of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas "Indigenous languages of the Americas")

See also: [American Indian English](/wiki/American_Indian_English "American Indian English")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Langs_N.Amer.png/500px-Langs_N.Amer.png)](/wiki/File:Langs_N.Amer.png)

Pre-contact: distribution of North American language families, including northern Mexico

The [Na-Dené](/wiki/Na-Den%C3%A9_languages "Na-Dené languages"), [Algic](/wiki/Algic_languages "Algic languages"), and [Uto-Aztecan](/wiki/Uto-Aztecan_languages "Uto-Aztecan languages") language families are the largest by number of languages. Uto-Aztecan has the most speakers (2 million) if the languages in Mexico are considered; Na-Dené comes second with approximately 200,000 speakers, and Algic third with about 180,000 speakers. Na-Dené and Algic have the widest geographic distributions: Algic spans from northeast Canada across much of the continent down to northeast Mexico with two outliers in [California](/wiki/California "California") ([Yurok](/wiki/Yurok_language "Yurok language") and [Wiyot](/wiki/Wiyot_language "Wiyot language")); Na-Dené spans from Alaska and west Canada through [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), and California to the [U.S. Southwest](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States") and northern Mexico. Several families consist of only 2 languages. Demonstrating genetic relationships has proved difficult due to the linguistic diversity present in North America. Two large (super-) family proposals, [Penutian](/wiki/Penutian_languages "Penutian languages") and [Hokan](/wiki/Hokan_languages "Hokan languages") have potential.

Words used in English have been [derived from Native American languages](/wiki/List_of_English_words_from_Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas "List of English words from Indigenous languages of the Americas").

#### Language education

See also: [Massachusett language § Current status](/wiki/Massachusett_language#Current_status "Massachusett language")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Cherokeeclass.png/250px-Cherokeeclass.png)](/wiki/File:Cherokeeclass.png)

[Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") Cherokee language immersion school student writing in the [Cherokee syllabary](/wiki/Cherokee_syllabary "Cherokee syllabary")

To counteract a shift to English, some Native American tribes have initiated language immersion schools for children, where an Indigenous American language is the medium of instruction. The [Cherokee Nation](/wiki/Cherokee_Nation "Cherokee Nation") initiated a 10-year language preservation plan that involved raising new fluent speakers of the [Cherokee language](/wiki/Cherokee_language "Cherokee language") from childhood up through school immersion programs, as well as community effort to continue to use the language at home.[[186]](#cite_note-188) This plan was part of a goal that, in 50 years, will result in 80% or more of the Cherokee being fluent.[[187]](#cite_note-preservation-189) The [Cherokee Preservation Foundation](/wiki/Cherokee_Preservation_Foundation "Cherokee Preservation Foundation") has invested $3 million in opening schools, training teachers, and developing curricula for language education, as well as initiating community gatherings where the language can be actively used.[[187]](#cite_note-preservation-189) Formed in 2006, the Kituwah Preservation & Education Program (KPEP) on the [Qualla Boundary](/wiki/Qualla_Boundary "Qualla Boundary") focuses on language immersion programs for children from birth to [fifth grade](/wiki/Fifth_grade "Fifth grade"), developing cultural resources for the public and community language programs to foster the Cherokee language among adults.[[188]](#cite_note-kpep-190)

There is a Cherokee language immersion school in [Tahlequah, Oklahoma](/wiki/Tahlequah,_Oklahoma "Tahlequah, Oklahoma"), that educates students from pre-school through eighth grade.[[189]](#cite_note-191) Because Oklahoma's official language is English, Cherokee immersion students are hindered when taking state-mandated tests because they can have little competence in English.[[190]](#cite_note-immersion-192) The Department of Education of Oklahoma said that in 2012 state tests: 11% of the school's sixth-graders showed proficiency in math, and 25% in reading; 31% of the seventh-graders showed proficiency in math, and 87% in reading; 50% of eighth-graders showed proficiency in math, and 78% in reading.[[190]](#cite_note-immersion-192) The Department of Education listed the charter school as a Targeted Intervention school, meaning it was identified as a low-performing school.[[190]](#cite_note-immersion-192) Ultimately, the school made a C, or a 2.33 grade point average on the state's A-F report card system.[[190]](#cite_note-immersion-192) The report card shows the school getting an F in mathematics achievement and mathematics growth, a C in social studies achievement, a D in reading achievement, and an A in reading growth and student attendance.[[190]](#cite_note-immersion-192) "The C we made is tremendous", said school principal Holly Davis, "[t]here is no English instruction in our school's younger grades, and we gave them this test in English."[[190]](#cite_note-immersion-192) She had anticipated the low grade because it was the school's first year as a state-funded [charter school](/wiki/Charter_school "Charter school"), and many had difficulty with English.[[190]](#cite_note-immersion-192) Eighth graders who graduate from the Tahlequah immersion school are fluent speakers of the language, and they usually go on to attend [Sequoyah High School](/wiki/Sequoyah_High_School_(Tahlequah,_Oklahoma) "Sequoyah High School (Tahlequah, Oklahoma)") where classes are taught in English and Cherokee.

### Indigenous foodways

Further information: [Indigenous cuisine of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_cuisine_of_the_Americas "Indigenous cuisine of the Americas"), [Inuit cuisine](/wiki/Inuit_cuisine "Inuit cuisine"), and [Eastern Agricultural Complex](/wiki/Eastern_Agricultural_Complex "Eastern Agricultural Complex")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/1940_govt_photo_minnesota_farming_scene_chippewa_baby_teething_on_magazine_indians_at_work.jpg/250px-1940_govt_photo_minnesota_farming_scene_chippewa_baby_teething_on_magazine_indians_at_work.jpg)](/wiki/File:1940_govt_photo_minnesota_farming_scene_chippewa_baby_teething_on_magazine_indians_at_work.jpg)

[Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe") baby waits on a [cradleboard](/wiki/Cradleboard "Cradleboard") while parents tend [wild rice](/wiki/Wild_rice "Wild rice") crops ([Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), 1940).

Historical diets of Native Americans differed dramatically from region to region. Different peoples might have relied more heavily on agriculture, horticulture, hunting, fishing, or gathering wild plants and fungi. Tribes developed diets best suited to their environments.

[Iñupiat](/wiki/I%C3%B1upiat "Iñupiat"), [Yupiit](/wiki/Yupiit "Yupiit"), [Unangan](/wiki/Unangan "Unangan"), and fellow [Alaska Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives") fished, hunted, and harvested wild plants, but did not rely on agriculture. Coastal peoples relied more heavily on sea mammals, fish, and fish eggs, while inland peoples hunted [caribou](/wiki/Caribou "Caribou") and [moose](/wiki/Moose "Moose").[[191]](#cite_note-193) Alaskan Natives prepared and preserved dried and smoked meat and fish.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Frybread_taco.jpg/250px-Frybread_taco.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frybread_taco.jpg)

[Frybread](/wiki/Frybread "Frybread"), made into an [Indian taco](/wiki/Taco#Indian_taco "Taco")

[Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest") tribes crafted seafaring [dugout canoes](/wiki/Dugout_canoe "Dugout canoe") 40–50 feet (12–15 m) long for fishing.
In the [Eastern Woodlands](/wiki/Eastern_Woodlands "Eastern Woodlands"), early peoples independently invented agricultural and by 1800 BCE developed the crops of the [Eastern Agricultural Complex](/wiki/Eastern_Agricultural_Complex "Eastern Agricultural Complex"), which include squash (*[Cucurbita pepo](/wiki/Cucurbita_pepo "Cucurbita pepo") ssp. ovifera*), sunflower (*[Helianthus annuus](/wiki/Helianthus_annuus "Helianthus annuus") var. macrocarpus*), goosefoot (*[Chenopodium berlandieri](/wiki/Chenopodium_berlandieri "Chenopodium berlandieri")*), and marsh elder (*[Iva annua](/wiki/Iva_annua "Iva annua") var. macrocarpa*).[[192]](#cite_note-194)[[193]](#cite_note-195)

The [Sonoran Desert](/wiki/Sonoran_Desert "Sonoran Desert") region including parts of [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") and [California](/wiki/California "California"), part of a region known as [Aridoamerica](/wiki/Aridoamerica "Aridoamerica"), relied on the [tepary bean](/wiki/Tepary_bean "Tepary bean") as a staple crop. This and other desert crops, [mesquite](/wiki/Mesquite "Mesquite") bead pods, *tunas* ([prickly pear](/wiki/Opuntia_ficus-indica "Opuntia ficus-indica") fruit), cholla buds, [saguaro](/wiki/Saguaro "Saguaro") cactus fruit, and [acorns](/wiki/Acorn#Use_by_Native_Americans "Acorn") are actively promoted by Tohono O'odham Community Action.[[194]](#cite_note-196) In the Southwest, some communities developed [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation") techniques while others, such as the [Hopi](/wiki/Hopi "Hopi") dry-farmed. They filled storehouses with grain as protection against the area's frequent [droughts](/wiki/Droughts "Droughts").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Zea_mays.jpg/250px-Zea_mays.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zea_mays.jpg)

[Maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") grown by Native Americans

[Maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize") or [corn](/wiki/Corn "Corn"), first cultivated in what is now Mexico, was traded north into Aridoamerica and [Oasisamerica](/wiki/Oasisamerica "Oasisamerica"), [southwest](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States"). Maize cultivation spread throughout the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains") and [Eastern Woodlands](/wiki/Eastern_Woodlands "Eastern Woodlands") by 200 CE. Native farmers practiced [polycropping](/wiki/Polyculture "Polyculture") maize, beans, and squash; these crops are known as the [Three Sisters](/wiki/Three_Sisters_(agriculture) "Three Sisters (agriculture)"). The beans would replace the [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen"), which the maize leached from the ground, as well as using corn stalks for support for climbing. The deficiencies of a diet dependent on maize were mitigated by the practice of converting maize kernels into [hominy](/wiki/Hominy "Hominy") in a process called [Nixtamalization](/wiki/Nixtamalization "Nixtamalization").[[195]](#cite_note-Johnson|Marston-197)

The [agriculture gender roles](/wiki/Gender_roles_in_agriculture "Gender roles in agriculture") of the Native Americans varied from region to region. In the Southwest area, men prepared the soil with [hoes](/wiki/Hoe_(tool) "Hoe (tool)"). The women were in charge of [planting](/wiki/Planting "Planting"), [weeding](/wiki/Weeding "Weeding"), and [harvesting](/wiki/Harvesting "Harvesting"). In most other regions, the women were in charge of most agriculture, including clearing the land, which was an immense chore, as they rotated fields. Europeans in the eastern part of the continent observed that Native Americans cleared large areas for cropland. Their fields in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") sometimes covered hundreds of acres. Colonists in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") noted thousands of acres under cultivation by Native Americans.[[196]](#cite_note-Krech-198)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/The_King_of_the_Seas_in_the_Hands_of_the_Makahs_-_1910.jpg/250px-The_King_of_the_Seas_in_the_Hands_of_the_Makahs_-_1910.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_King_of_the_Seas_in_the_Hands_of_the_Makahs_-_1910.jpg)

[Makah](/wiki/Makah_people "Makah people") Native Americans and a whale, *The King of the Seas in the Hands of the Makahs*, 1910 photograph by [Asahel Curtis](/wiki/Asahel_Curtis "Asahel Curtis")

Early farmers commonly used tools such as the [hoe](/wiki/Hoe_(tool) "Hoe (tool)"), [maul](/wiki/Sledgehammer "Sledgehammer"), and [dibber](/wiki/Dibber "Dibber"). The hoe was the main tool used to till the land and prepare it for planting; then it was used for weeding. The first versions were made out of [wood](/wiki/Wood "Wood") and [stone](/wiki/Stone "Stone"). When the settlers brought [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"), Native Americans switched to iron hoes and [hatchets](/wiki/Hatchets "Hatchets"). The dibber was a digging stick, used to plant the seed. Once the plants were harvested, women prepared the produce for eating. They used the maul to grind the corn into a mash. It was cooked and eaten that way or baked as cornbread.[[197]](#cite_note-199)

### Religion

Main article: [Native American religions](/wiki/Native_American_religions "Native American religions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Baptism_of_Pocahontas.jpg/250px-Baptism_of_Pocahontas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baptism_of_Pocahontas.jpg)

*[Baptism of Pocahontas](/wiki/Baptism_of_Pocahontas "Baptism of Pocahontas")*, painted in 1840 by [John Gadsby Chapman](/wiki/John_Gadsby_Chapman "John Gadsby Chapman")

Native American religious practices and beliefs, differ widely across tribes. These [spiritualities](/wiki/Spirituality "Spirituality"), practices, beliefs, may accompany adherence to another faith or can represent a person's primary religious, faith, spiritual or philosophical identity. Much Native American spirituality exists in a tribal-cultural continuum, so cannot be easily separated from tribal identity itself.

Some tribes include the use of sacred leaves and herbs such as tobacco, [sweetgrass](/wiki/Hierochloe_odorata "Hierochloe odorata") or [sage](/wiki/Leucophyllum "Leucophyllum"). Many Plains tribes have [sweatlodge](/wiki/Sweatlodge "Sweatlodge") ceremonies, though the specifics vary among tribes. Fasting, singing, prayer, and [drumming](/wiki/Native_American_music "Native American music") are common.[[198]](#cite_note-200)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Saint_John_Neumann_Catholic_Church_%28Sunbury%2C_Ohio%29_-_interior%2C_statue_of_Saint_Kateri_Tekakwitha.jpg/250px-Saint_John_Neumann_Catholic_Church_%28Sunbury%2C_Ohio%29_-_interior%2C_statue_of_Saint_Kateri_Tekakwitha.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_John_Neumann_Catholic_Church_(Sunbury,_Ohio)_-_interior,_statue_of_Saint_Kateri_Tekakwitha.jpg)

[Saint Kateri Tekakwitha](/wiki/Saint_Kateri_Tekakwitha "Saint Kateri Tekakwitha"), the patron of [ecologists](/wiki/Ecologist "Ecologist"), [exiles](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/exile#Noun "wikt:exile"), and orphans, was canonized by the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church")

The [Midewiwin Lodge](/wiki/Midewiwin "Midewiwin") is a medicine society inspired by the oral history and prophesies of the [Ojibwa](/wiki/Ojibwa "Ojibwa") (Chippewa) and related tribes.

Another significant religious body among Native peoples is known as the [Native American Church](/wiki/Native_American_Church "Native American Church"). It is a [syncretistic](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") church incorporating elements of Native spiritual practice from different tribes as well as symbolic elements from [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"). Its main rite is the [peyote](/wiki/Peyote "Peyote") ceremony. Prior to 1890, traditional religious beliefs included [Wakan Tanka](/wiki/Wakan_Tanka "Wakan Tanka"). In the American Southwest, especially [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), a syncretism between the Catholicism brought by Spanish missionaries and the native religion is common; the religious drums, chants, and dances of the [Pueblo people](/wiki/Pueblo_people "Pueblo people") are regularly part of [Masses](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") at [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico")'s [Saint Francis Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Francis_Cathedral "Saint Francis Cathedral").[[199]](#cite_note-201) Native American-Catholic syncretism is also found elsewhere in the US. Some Native American tribes who practice Christianity, including the [Lumbee](/wiki/Lumbee "Lumbee"), organized denominations, such as the [Lumber River Conference of the Holiness Methodist Church](/wiki/Lumber_River_Conference_of_the_Holiness_Methodist_Church "Lumber River Conference of the Holiness Methodist Church").[[200]](#cite_note-Melton2003-202)

The [eagle feather law](/wiki/Eagle_feather_law "Eagle feather law") stipulates that only individuals of certifiable Native American ancestry, enrolled in a federally recognized tribe, are legally authorized to obtain [eagle](/wiki/Eagle "Eagle") feathers for religious or spiritual use. The law does not allow Native Americans to give eagle feathers to non-Native Americans.[[201]](#cite_note-203)

### Gender roles

Main articles: [Gender roles among the Indigenous peoples of North America](/wiki/Gender_roles_among_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_North_America "Gender roles among the Indigenous peoples of North America"), [Clan Mother](/wiki/Clan_Mother_(disambiguation) "Clan Mother (disambiguation)"), [Matriarchy](/wiki/Matriarchy "Matriarchy"), [Matrilineality](/wiki/Matrilineality "Matrilineality"), and [Two-Spirit](/wiki/Two-Spirit "Two-Spirit")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Doctor.susan.la.flesche.picotte.jpg/250px-Doctor.susan.la.flesche.picotte.jpg)](/wiki/File:Doctor.susan.la.flesche.picotte.jpg)

[Susan La Flesche Picotte](/wiki/Susan_La_Flesche_Picotte "Susan La Flesche Picotte") was the first Native American woman to become a [physician](/wiki/Physician "Physician") in the United States.

Gender roles are differentiated in many Native American tribes. Many Natives have retained traditional expectations of sexuality and gender and continue to do so in contemporary life despite ongoing colonial pressures.[[202]](#cite_note-Estrada-204)

Whether a particular tribe is predominantly [matrilineal](/wiki/Matrilineality "Matrilineality") or [patrilineal](/wiki/Patrilineal "Patrilineal"), often both sexes have some degree of decision-making power within the tribe. Many Nations, such as the [Haudenosaunee](/wiki/Haudenosaunee "Haudenosaunee") Five Nations and the Southeast Muskogean tribes, have matrilineal or [Clan Mother](/wiki/Haudenosaunee_Clan_Mother "Haudenosaunee Clan Mother") systems, in which property and hereditary leadership are controlled by and passed through the maternal lines.[[203]](#cite_note-Thomas-205) In these Nations, the children are considered to belong to the mother's clan. In [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee_people "Cherokee people") culture, women own the family property. When traditional young women marry, their husbands may join them in their mother's household.

Matrilineal structures enable young women to have assistance in childbirth and rearing and protect them in case of conflicts between the couple. If a couple separates or the man dies, the woman has her family to assist her. In matrilineal cultures the mother's brothers are usually the leading male figures in her children's lives; fathers have no standing in their wife and children's clan, as they still belong to their mother's clan. Hereditary clan chief positions pass through the mother's line and chiefs have historically been selected on the recommendations of women elders, who could also disapprove of a chief.[[203]](#cite_note-Thomas-205)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/George_Catlin_-_Ball-play_of_the_Women%2C_Prairie_du_Chien_-_1985.66.430_-_Smithsonian_American_Art_Museum.jpg/250px-George_Catlin_-_Ball-play_of_the_Women%2C_Prairie_du_Chien_-_1985.66.430_-_Smithsonian_American_Art_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Catlin_-_Ball-play_of_the_Women,_Prairie_du_Chien_-_1985.66.430_-_Smithsonian_American_Art_Museum.jpg)

Ball-play of the women, painting by [George Catlin](/wiki/George_Catlin "George Catlin"), c. 1835

In the [patrilineal](/wiki/Patrilineal "Patrilineal") tribes, such as the [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha "Omaha"), [Osage](/wiki/Osage_Nation "Osage Nation"), [Ponca](/wiki/Ponca "Ponca"), and [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people"), hereditary leadership passes through the male line, and children are considered to belong to the father and his [clan](/wiki/Clan "Clan"). In patrilineal tribes, if a woman marries a non-Native, she is no longer considered part of the tribe, and her children are considered to share the ethnicity and culture of their father.[[204]](#cite_note-TrueLogan-206)

In patriarchal tribes, gender roles tend to be rigid. Men have historically hunted, traded and made war while women have primary responsibility for the survival and welfare of the families (and future of the tribe). Women usually gather and cultivate plants, use plants and herbs to treat illnesses, care for the young and the elderly, make clothing and instruments, and process and cure meat and skins from the game. Some mothers use [cradleboards](/wiki/Cradleboard "Cradleboard") to carry an infant while working or traveling.[[205]](#cite_note-207) In matriarchal and egalitarian nations, the gender roles are usually not so clear-cut and even less so in the modern era.[[202]](#cite_note-Estrada-204) Several dozen tribes allowed [polygyny](/wiki/Polygyny "Polygyny") to sisters, with procedural and economic limits.[[182]](#cite_note-Morgan1907-184)

[Lakota, Dakota, and Nakota](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux") girls are encouraged to learn to ride, hunt and fight.[[206]](#cite_note-208) Though fighting in war has mostly been left to the boys and men, occasionally women have fought, in battles and defense of the home, especially if the tribe was severely threatened.[[207]](#cite_note-209)

### Modern education

As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Native_Americans_in_the_United_States&action=edit) 90% of Native American school-aged children attend public schools operated by school districts.[[208]](#cite_note-Woodsgetsaway-210) Tribally-operated schools under contracts/grants with the [Bureau of Indian Education](/wiki/Bureau_of_Indian_Education "Bureau of Indian Education") (BIE) and direct BIE-operated schools take about 8% of Native American students,[[209]](#cite_note-211) including students who live in very rural remote areas.[[208]](#cite_note-Woodsgetsaway-210)

In 1978, 215,000 (78%) of Native Americans attended school district-operated public schools, 47,000 (17%) attended schools directly operated by the BIA, 2,500 (1%) attended tribal or other schools that contracted with the BIA, and the remaining 9,000 (3%) attended missionary schools for Native American children or other private schools.[[210]](#cite_note-212)

### Sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Jim_Thorpe_1913b-cr.jpg/250px-Jim_Thorpe_1913b-cr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jim_Thorpe_1913b-cr.jpg)

[Jim Thorpe](/wiki/Jim_Thorpe "Jim Thorpe"), gold medalist at the 1912 Olympics, in the [pentathlon](/wiki/Pentathlon "Pentathlon") and [decathlon](/wiki/Decathlon "Decathlon") events

Native American leisure time led to competitive individual and team sports. [Jim Thorpe](/wiki/Jim_Thorpe "Jim Thorpe"), [Lewis Tewanima](/wiki/Lewis_Tewanima "Lewis Tewanima"), [Joe Hipp](/wiki/Joe_Hipp "Joe Hipp"), [Notah Begay III](/wiki/Notah_Begay_III "Notah Begay III"), [Chris Wondolowski](/wiki/Chris_Wondolowski "Chris Wondolowski"), [Jacoby Ellsbury](/wiki/Jacoby_Ellsbury "Jacoby Ellsbury"), [Joba Chamberlain](/wiki/Joba_Chamberlain "Joba Chamberlain"), [Kyle Lohse](/wiki/Kyle_Lohse "Kyle Lohse"), [Sam Bradford](/wiki/Sam_Bradford "Sam Bradford"), [Jack Brisco](/wiki/Jack_Brisco "Jack Brisco"), [Tommy Morrison](/wiki/Tommy_Morrison "Tommy Morrison"), [Billy Mills](/wiki/Billy_Mills "Billy Mills"), [Angel Goodrich](/wiki/Angel_Goodrich "Angel Goodrich"), [Shoni Schimmel](/wiki/Shoni_Schimmel "Shoni Schimmel"), and [Kyrie Irving](/wiki/Kyrie_Irving "Kyrie Irving") are well known professional athletes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Ball_players.jpg/250px-Ball_players.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ball_players.jpg)

Ball players from the [Choctaw](/wiki/Choctaw "Choctaw") and [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people") tribe in a 19th-century [lithograph](/wiki/Lithography "Lithography") by [George Catlin](/wiki/George_Catlin "George Catlin")

#### Team sports

Native American ball sports, sometimes referred to as [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse"), stickball, or baggataway, were often used to settle disputes, rather than war, as a civil way to settle conflict. The [Choctaw](/wiki/Choctaw "Choctaw") called it *isitoboli* ("Little Brother of War");[[211]](#cite_note-choctaw_stickball-213) the [Onondaga](/wiki/Onondaga_language "Onondaga language") name was *dehuntshigwa'es* ("men hit a rounded object"). There are three basic versions, classified as Great Lakes, Iroquoian, and Southern.[[212]](#cite_note-three_stickball-214)

The game is played with one or two rackets or sticks and one ball. The object is to land the ball in the opposing team's goal (either a single post or net) and prevent the opposing team from scoring. The game involves 20 or as many as 300 players with no height or weight restrictions, or protective gear. The goals could be from around 200 feet (61 m) apart to about 2 miles (3.2 km); in lacrosse the field is 110 yards (100 m).

#### Individual sports

[Chunkey](/wiki/Chunkey "Chunkey") was a game that consisted of a stone-shaped disk about 1–2 inches in diameter. The disk was thrown down a 200-foot (61 m) corridor so it could roll past the players at great speed. Players would throw wooden shafts at the moving disk. The object of the game was to strike the disk or prevent your opponents from hitting it.

#### U.S. Olympics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/BillyMills_Crossing_Finish_Line_1964Olympics.jpg/250px-BillyMills_Crossing_Finish_Line_1964Olympics.jpg)](/wiki/File:BillyMills_Crossing_Finish_Line_1964Olympics.jpg)

[Billy Mills](/wiki/Billy_Mills "Billy Mills") crosses the finish line at the end of the 10,000-meter race at the [1964 Tokyo Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics").

[Jim Thorpe](/wiki/Jim_Thorpe "Jim Thorpe"), a [Sauk and Fox](/wiki/Sauk_and_Fox "Sauk and Fox") Native American, was an all-around athlete playing football and baseball in the early 20th century. Future President [Dwight Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_Eisenhower "Dwight Eisenhower") injured his knee while trying to tackle the young Thorpe. In a 1961 speech, Eisenhower recalled Thorpe: "Here and there, there are some people who are supremely endowed. My memory goes back to Jim Thorpe. He never practiced in his life, and he could do anything better than any other football player I ever saw."[[213]](#cite_note-CNN-215)

In the 1912 Olympics, Thorpe ran the 100-yard dash in 10 seconds flat, the 220 in 21.8 seconds, the 440 in 51.8 seconds, the 880 in 1:57, the mile in 4:35, the 120-yard high hurdles in 15 seconds, and the 220-yard low hurdles in 24 seconds.[[214]](#cite_note-NYTobit-216) He long jumped 23 ft 6 in and high-jumped 6 ft 5 in.[[214]](#cite_note-NYTobit-216) He [pole vaulted](/wiki/Pole_vault "Pole vault") 11 feet (3.4 m), [put the shot](/wiki/Shot_put "Shot put") 47 ft 9 in (14.55 m), [throw the javelin](/wiki/Javelin_throw "Javelin throw") 163 feet (50 m), and the [discus](/wiki/Discus_throw "Discus throw") 136 feet (41 m).[[214]](#cite_note-NYTobit-216) Thorpe entered the U.S. Olympic trials for the pentathlon and the decathlon.

[Louis Tewanima](/wiki/Louis_Tewanima "Louis Tewanima"), [Hopi people](/wiki/Hopi_people "Hopi people"), was an American two-time Olympic distance runner and silver medalist in the 10,000-meters in 1912. He ran for the Carlisle Indian School where he was a teammate of Thorpe. His silver in 1912 remained the best US achievement in this event until another Indian, Billy Mills, won gold in 1964. Tewanima competed at the 1908 Olympics, where he finished 9th in the marathon.

[Ellison Brown](/wiki/Ellison_Brown "Ellison Brown"), of the [Narragansett people](/wiki/Narragansett_people "Narragansett people") from Rhode Island, won two Boston Marathons (1936, 1939) and competed in the 1936 Olympics in Berlin, but did not finish due to injury. He qualified for the 1940 Olympic Games in Helsinki, but the games were canceled due to the outbreak of World War II.

[Billy Mills](/wiki/Billy_Mills "Billy Mills"), a [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people") and [USMC](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") officer, won gold in the 10,000-meters at the [1964 Tokyo Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics"). He was the only American ever to win the gold in this event.

[Billy Kidd](/wiki/Billy_Kidd "Billy Kidd"), part [Abenaki](/wiki/Abenaki "Abenaki") from [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"), became the first American man to medal in [alpine skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing "Alpine skiing") in the Olympics, taking silver in the [slalom](/wiki/Slalom_skiing "Slalom skiing") in the [1964 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1964_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the 1964 Winter Olympics") at [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck"), Austria. Six years later at the 1970 World Championships, Kidd won gold in the [combined](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_combined "Alpine skiing combined") event and bronze in the slalom.

[Ashton Locklear](/wiki/Ashton_Locklear "Ashton Locklear") ([Lumbee](/wiki/Lumbee_people "Lumbee people")), an uneven bars specialist, was an alternate for the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") U.S. gymnastics team, the [Final Five](/wiki/Final_Five_(gymnastics) "Final Five (gymnastics)").[[215]](#cite_note-ashloc-217) In 2016, [Kyrie Irving](/wiki/Kyrie_Irving "Kyrie Irving") ([Sioux](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux")) also helped Team USA win gold at the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Basketball at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament"). He became the 4th member of Team USA to capture the NBA championship and Olympic gold in the same year, joining [LeBron James](/wiki/LeBron_James "LeBron James"), [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan"), and [Scottie Pippen](/wiki/Scottie_Pippen "Scottie Pippen").[[216]](#cite_note-218)

### Literature

Main article: [Native American literature](/wiki/Native_American_literature "Native American literature")

See also: [List of writers from peoples Indigenous to the Americas](/wiki/List_of_writers_from_peoples_Indigenous_to_the_Americas "List of writers from peoples Indigenous to the Americas") and [Postcolonial literature](/wiki/Postcolonial_literature "Postcolonial literature")

Native American literature, composed of [oral](/wiki/Oral_literature "Oral literature") and written literature, has a long history. It is considered a series of literatures reflecting the varied traditions and histories of different tribes. Modern authors cover a range of genres and include [Tommy Orange](/wiki/Tommy_Orange "Tommy Orange"), [Joy Harjo](/wiki/Joy_Harjo "Joy Harjo"), [Louise Erdrich](/wiki/Louise_Erdrich "Louise Erdrich"), [Stephen Graham Jones](/wiki/Stephen_Graham_Jones "Stephen Graham Jones"), [Rebecca Roanhorse](/wiki/Rebecca_Roanhorse "Rebecca Roanhorse"), [Tommy Pico](/wiki/Tommy_Pico "Tommy Pico"), and many more.

### Music

Main article: [Native American music](/wiki/Native_American_music "Native American music")

See also: [Native American hip hop](/wiki/Native_American_hip_hop "Native American hip hop") and [Indigenous metal music](/wiki/Indigenous_metal_music "Indigenous metal music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/UIATF_Pow_Wow_2009_-_023.jpg/250px-UIATF_Pow_Wow_2009_-_023.jpg)](/wiki/File:UIATF_Pow_Wow_2009_-_023.jpg)

[Fancy Dancer](/wiki/Fancy_dance "Fancy dance") at the Seafair Indian Days [Pow-Wow](/wiki/Pow-Wow "Pow-Wow"), [Daybreak Star Cultural Center](/wiki/Daybreak_Star_Cultural_Center "Daybreak Star Cultural Center"), [Seattle, Washington](/wiki/Seattle,_Washington "Seattle, Washington")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Jake_fragua_jemez_pueblo.jpg/250px-Jake_fragua_jemez_pueblo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jake_fragua_jemez_pueblo.jpg)

Jake Fragua, [Jemez Pueblo](/wiki/Jemez_Pueblo "Jemez Pueblo") from [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico")

Traditional Native American music is almost entirely [monophonic](/wiki/Texture_(music) "Texture (music)"), but there are exceptions. It often includes [drums](/wiki/Drums "Drums"), rattles, or other percussion, but little other instrumentation. [Flutes](/wiki/Native_American_flute "Native American flute") and whistles made of wood, cane, or bone are also played, generally by individuals, but in former times also by large ensembles, as noted by [conquistador](/wiki/Conquistador "Conquistador") [de Soto](/wiki/Hernando_de_Soto_(explorer) "Hernando de Soto (explorer)"). The tuning of modern flutes is typically [pentatonic](/wiki/Pentatonic "Pentatonic").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Performers with Native American parentage have occasionally appeared in American popular music such as [Rita Coolidge](/wiki/Rita_Coolidge "Rita Coolidge"), [Wayne Newton](/wiki/Wayne_Newton "Wayne Newton"), [Gene Clark](/wiki/Gene_Clark "Gene Clark"), [Blackfoot](/wiki/Blackfoot_(band) "Blackfoot (band)"), and [Redbone](/wiki/Redbone_(band) "Redbone (band)"). Some, such as [John Trudell](/wiki/John_Trudell "John Trudell"), have used music to comment on life in Native America. Others such as [R. Carlos Nakai](/wiki/R._Carlos_Nakai "R. Carlos Nakai"), [Joanne Shenandoah](/wiki/Joanne_Shenandoah "Joanne Shenandoah") and [Robert "Tree" Cody](/wiki/Robert_%22Tree%22_Cody "Robert \"Tree\" Cody") integrate traditional sounds with modern, in instrumental recordings, whereas music by [Charles Littleleaf](/wiki/Charles_Littleleaf "Charles Littleleaf") is derived from ancestral heritage as well as nature. Recording companies offer an abundance of music by contemporary Native American performers young and old, ranging from [pow-wow](/wiki/Pow-wow "Pow-wow") drum music to rock-and-roll and rap. In the ballet dancing [Maria Tallchief](/wiki/Maria_Tallchief "Maria Tallchief") was considered America's first major [prima ballerina](/wiki/Prima_ballerina "Prima ballerina"),[[217]](#cite_note-SunTimes_obit-219) and the first of Native American descent to hold the rank.[[218]](#cite_note-Time_obit-220) Along with her sister [Marjorie Tallchief](/wiki/Marjorie_Tallchief "Marjorie Tallchief") both became star ballerinas.

The most widely practiced public musical form among Native Americans is that of the [pow-wow](/wiki/Pow-wow "Pow-wow"). At pow-wows, such as the annual [Gathering of Nations](/wiki/Gathering_of_Nations "Gathering of Nations") in [Albuquerque, New Mexico](/wiki/Albuquerque,_New_Mexico "Albuquerque, New Mexico"), members of drum groups sit in a circle around a large drum. Drum groups play in unison while they sing and dancers in colorful regalia dance clockwise around the drum groups. Familiar pow-wow songs include honor songs, intertribal songs, crow-hops, sneak-up songs, grass-dances, two-steps, welcome songs, going-home songs, and war songs. Most Indigenous communities maintain traditional songs and ceremonies, some of which are shared and practiced exclusively within the community.[[219]](#cite_note-221)

### Art

Further information: [petroglyph](/wiki/Petroglyph "Petroglyph"), [pictogram](/wiki/Pictogram "Pictogram"), [petroform](/wiki/Petroform "Petroform"), [Visual arts by indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Visual_arts_by_indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Visual arts by indigenous peoples of the Americas"), [indigenous ceramics of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_ceramics_of_the_Americas "Indigenous ceramics of the Americas"), and [Native American jewelry](/wiki/Native_American_jewelry "Native American jewelry")

The [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), living around the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") and extending east and north, used strings or belts called *[wampum](/wiki/Wampum "Wampum")* that served a dual function: the knots and beaded designs mnemonically chronicled tribal stories and legends, and served as a medium of exchange and unit of measure. The keepers of the articles were seen as tribal dignitaries.[[220]](#cite_note-222)

[Pueblo peoples](/wiki/Pueblo_peoples "Pueblo peoples") crafted impressive items associated with their religious ceremonies. *[Kachina](/wiki/Kachina "Kachina")* dancers wore elaborately painted and decorated masks as they ritually impersonated ancestral spirits.[[221]](#cite_note-223) Pueblo people are noted for high-quality pottery, often with geometric designs and floral, animal and bird motifs.[[222]](#cite_note-224) Carved stone and wood [fetishes](/wiki/Fetishism "Fetishism") were made for religious use. Superior weaving, embroidered decorations, and rich dyes characterized their textile arts. Turquoise and shell jewelry were created, as were formalized pictorial arts.[[223]](#cite_note-225)

[Navajo](/wiki/Navajo "Navajo") spirituality focused on the maintenance of a harmonious relationship with the spirit world, often achieved by ceremonial acts, usually incorporating [sandpainting](/wiki/Sandpainting "Sandpainting"). For the Navajo, sand painting is not just a representational object, but a spiritual entity with a life of its own, which helped the patient at the center of the ceremony re-establish a connection with the life force. These sand creations were erased at the end of the healing ceremony.[[224]](#cite_note-226)

It has been estimated that the Native American arts and crafts industry brings in a billion USD in gross sales annually.[[225]](#cite_note-227) Native American art comprises a major category in the world art collection.[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*] Native American contributions include [pottery](/wiki/Native_American_pottery "Native American pottery"), [paintings](/wiki/Painting "Painting"), [jewellery](/wiki/Jewellery "Jewellery"), [weavings](/wiki/Weaving "Weaving"), [sculpture](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture"), [basketry](/wiki/Basketry "Basketry"), and [carvings](/wiki/Wood_carving "Wood carving"). The integrity of certain Native American artworks is protected by the [Indian Arts and Crafts Act of 1990](/wiki/Indian_Arts_and_Crafts_Act_of_1990 "Indian Arts and Crafts Act of 1990"), which prohibits the representation of art as Native American when it is not the product of an enrolled Native American artist. Gail Sheffield and others claim this has had "the unintended consequence of sanctioning discrimination against Native Americans whose tribal affiliation was not officially recognized".[[226]](#cite_note-228) Artists such as [Jeanne Rorex Bridges](/wiki/Jeanne_Rorex_Bridges "Jeanne Rorex Bridges") ([Echota Cherokee](/wiki/Echota_Cherokee_Tribe_of_Alabama "Echota Cherokee Tribe of Alabama")), who was not enrolled, ran the risk of fines or imprisonment if they sold their art while affirming their heritage.[[227]](#cite_note-229)[[228]](#cite_note-230)[[229]](#cite_note-231)

Notable Native American artists include [Franklin Gritts](/wiki/Franklin_Gritts "Franklin Gritts"), a Cherokee artist who taught students from many tribes at Haskell Institute (now [Haskell Indian Nations University](/wiki/Haskell_Indian_Nations_University "Haskell Indian Nations University")) in the 1940s, the *Golden Age* of Native American painters.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Interracial relations

Main article: [Interracial relations among Native Americans in the United States](/wiki/Interracial_relations_among_Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Interracial relations among Native Americans in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Portrait_%28Front%29_of_Lillian_Gross%2C_Niece_of_Susan_Sanders_%28Mixed_Blood%29_1906.jpg/250px-Portrait_%28Front%29_of_Lillian_Gross%2C_Niece_of_Susan_Sanders_%28Mixed_Blood%29_1906.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_(Front)_of_Lillian_Gross,_Niece_of_Susan_Sanders_(Mixed_Blood)_1906.jpg)

Lillian Gross, described as a "Mixed Blood" by the Smithsonian source,[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] was of Cherokee and European American heritage. She identified with the Cherokee culture in which she was raised.

Interracial relations between Native Americans, Europeans, and Africans is a complex issue that has been mostly neglected with "few in-depth studies on interracial relationships".[[230]](#cite_note-lin-232)[[231]](#cite_note-takingAssToHeart-233) Native Americans are more likely than any other racial group to practice [interracial or intertribal marriage](/wiki/Interracial_marriage_in_the_United_States "Interracial marriage in the United States") among the different tribes and non-Natives, resulting in an ever-declining proportion of Indigenous blood among those who claim a Native American identity (tribes often count only the Indian blood from their own tribal background in the enrollment process, disregarding intertribal heritages).[[232]](#cite_note-234)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Assimilation

Further information: [Cultural assimilation of Native Americans](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation_of_Native_Americans "Cultural assimilation of Native Americans")

European impact was immediate, widespread, and profound already during the early years of colonization and the creation of the countries which currently exist in the Americas. Europeans living among Native Americans were often called "white indians". They "lived in native communities for years, learned native languages fluently, attended native councils, and often fought alongside their native companions".[[233]](#cite_note-white_indians-235)

Early contact was often charged with tension and emotion, but also had moments of friendship, cooperation, and intimacy.[[234]](#cite_note-white_red_relations1-236) Marriages took place in English, French, Russian and Spanish colonies between Native Americans and Europeans though Native American women were also the victims of rape.[[235]](#cite_note-udayu-237)

There was fear on both sides, as the different peoples realized how different their societies were.[[234]](#cite_note-white_red_relations1-236) Many whites regarded Native people as "savages" because the Native people were not Protestant or Roman Catholic and therefore the Native people were not considered to be human beings.[[234]](#cite_note-white_red_relations1-236) The Native American author, Andrew J. Blackbird, wrote in his *History of the [Ottawa](/wiki/Odawa "Odawa") and [Chippewa](/wiki/Chippewa "Chippewa") Indians of Michigan* (1897), that white settlers introduced some immoralities into Native American tribes. Many Native Americans suffered because the Europeans introduced alcohol. Many Native people do not break down alcohol in the same way as people of Eurasian background. Many Native people were learning what their body could [tolerate](/wiki/Alcohol_tolerance#Alcohol_tolerance_in_different_ethnic_groups "Alcohol tolerance") of this new substance and died as a result of imbibing too much.[[234]](#cite_note-white_red_relations1-236)

Blackbird wrote:

> The Ottawas and Chippewas were quite virtuous in their primitive state, as there were no illegitimate children reported in our old traditions. But very lately this evil came to exist among the Ottawas-so lately that the second case among the Ottawas of 'Arbor Croche' is yet living in 1897. And from that time this evil came to be quite frequent, for immorality has been introduced among these people by evil white persons who bring their vices into the tribes.[[234]](#cite_note-white_red_relations1-236)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Fort-orleans-return.jpg/250px-Fort-orleans-return.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fort-orleans-return.jpg)

The 1725 return of an Osage bride from a trip to [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"). She was married to a French soldier.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Carl_Wimar_Abduction_of_Boones_Daughter_detail_Amon_Carter_Museum.jpg/250px-Carl_Wimar_Abduction_of_Boones_Daughter_detail_Amon_Carter_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carl_Wimar_Abduction_of_Boones_Daughter_detail_Amon_Carter_Museum.jpg)

*Five Indians and a Captive*, painted by [Carl Wimar](/wiki/Carl_Wimar "Carl Wimar"), 1855

The U.S. government had two purposes when making land agreements with Native Americans: to open up more land for white settlement,[[234]](#cite_note-white_red_relations1-236) and to "ease tensions" (in other words assimilate Native people to Eurasian social ways) between whites and Native Americans by forcing the Native Americans to use the land in the same way as did the whites—for subsistence farms.[[234]](#cite_note-white_red_relations1-236) The government used a variety of strategies to achieve these goals; many treaties required Native Americans to become farmers in order to keep their land.[[234]](#cite_note-white_red_relations1-236) Government officials often did not translate the documents which Native Americans were forced to sign, and native chiefs often had little or no idea what they were signing.[[234]](#cite_note-white_red_relations1-236)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Charles_eastman_smithsonian_gn_03462a.jpg/250px-Charles_eastman_smithsonian_gn_03462a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_eastman_smithsonian_gn_03462a.jpg)

[Charles Eastman](/wiki/Charles_Eastman "Charles Eastman") was one of the first Native Americans to become certified as a [medical doctor](/wiki/Medical_Doctor "Medical Doctor"), after he graduated from [Boston University](/wiki/Boston_University "Boston University").[[236]](#cite_note-238)[[237]](#cite_note-239)

For a Native American man to marry a white woman, he had to get consent of her parents, as long as "he can prove to support her as a white woman in a good home".[[238]](#cite_note-white_reds-240) In the early 19th century, the [Shawnee](/wiki/Shawnee "Shawnee") Tecumseh and blonde hair, blue-eyed Rebecca Galloway had an interracial affair. In the late 19th century, three European American middle-class women teachers at [Hampton Institute](/wiki/Hampton_Institute "Hampton Institute") married Native American men whom they had met as students.[[239]](#cite_note-white_red_marriages-241)

As European American women started working independently at missions and Indian schools in the western states, there were more opportunities for their meeting and developing relationships with Native American men. For instance, [Charles Eastman](/wiki/Charles_Eastman "Charles Eastman"), a man of European and [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people") origin whose father sent both his sons to [Dartmouth College](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College"), got his medical degree at [Boston University](/wiki/Boston_University "Boston University") and returned to the West to practice. He married [Elaine Goodale](/wiki/Elaine_Goodale "Elaine Goodale"), whom he met in South Dakota. He was the grandson of [Seth Eastman](/wiki/Seth_Eastman "Seth Eastman"), a military officer from Maine, and a chief's daughter. Goodale was a young European American teacher from Massachusetts and a reformer, who was appointed as the U.S. superintendent of Native American education for the reservations in the Dakota Territory. They had six children together.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## European enslavement

Main articles: [Slavery among Native Americans in the United States](/wiki/Slavery_among_Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Slavery among Native Americans in the United States") and [Slavery among Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Slavery_among_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Slavery among Indigenous peoples of the Americas")

The majority of Native American tribes did practice some form of slavery before the European introduction of African slavery into North America, but none exploited slave labor on a large scale. Most Native American tribes did not barter captives in the pre-colonial era, although they sometimes exchanged enslaved individuals with other tribes in peace gestures or in exchange for their own members.[[240]](#cite_note-laubchap1-242) When Europeans arrived as [colonists](/wiki/Colonists "Colonists") in North America, Native Americans changed their practice of [slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") dramatically. Native Americans began selling war captives to Europeans rather than integrating them into their own societies as they had done before. As the demand for labor in the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies") grew with the cultivation of [sugar cane](/wiki/Sugar_cane "Sugar cane"), Europeans enslaved Native Americans for the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies"), and some were exported to the "sugar islands". The British settlers, especially those in the southern colonies, purchased or captured Native Americans to use as forced labor in cultivating tobacco, rice, and indigo. Accurate records of the numbers enslaved do not exist because vital statistics and census reports were at best infrequent.[[241]](#cite_note-laub-243) Scholars estimate tens to hundreds of thousands of Native Americans may have been enslaved by the Europeans, being sold by Native Americans themselves or Europeans.[[242]](#cite_note-244)[[243]](#cite_note-:6-245)

In Colonial America, slavery soon became [racialized](/wiki/Racialization "Racialization"), with those enslaved by the institution consisting of ethnic groups (non-Christian Native Americans and Africans) who were foreign to the Christian, European colonists. The [House of Burgesses](/wiki/House_of_Burgesses "House of Burgesses") define the terms of [slavery in Virginia](/wiki/History_of_slavery_in_Virginia "History of slavery in Virginia") in 1705:

> All servants imported and brought into the Country ... who were not Christians in their native Country ... shall be accounted and be slaves. All Negro, mulatto and Indian slaves within this dominion ... shall be held to be real estate. If any slave resists his master ... correcting such slave, and shall happen to be killed in such correction ... the master shall be free of all punishment ... as if such accident never happened.

— Virginia General Assembly declaration, 1705[[244]](#cite_note-pbsafna-246)

The slave trade of Native Americans lasted only until around 1750. It gave rise to a series of devastating wars among the tribes, including the [Yamasee War](/wiki/Yamasee_War "Yamasee War"). The [Indian Wars](/wiki/Indian_Wars "Indian Wars") of the early 18th century, combined with the increasing importation of African slaves, effectively ended the Native American slave trade by 1750. Colonists found that Native American slaves could easily escape, as they knew the country. The wars cost the lives of numerous colonial slave traders and disrupted their early societies. The remaining Native American groups banded together to face the Europeans from a position of strength. Many surviving Native American peoples of the southeast strengthened their loose coalitions of language groups and joined confederacies such as the [Choctaw](/wiki/Choctaw "Choctaw"), the [Creek](/wiki/Muscogee_(Creek) "Muscogee (Creek)"), and the [Catawba](/wiki/Catawba_(tribe) "Catawba (tribe)") for protection. Even after the Indian Slave Trade ended in 1750, the enslavement of Native Americans continued (mostly through kidnappings) in the west and in the [Southern states](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States").[[245]](#cite_note-ism-247)[[246]](#cite_note-248) Both Native American and African enslaved women suffered rape and sexual harassment by male slaveholders and other white men.[[235]](#cite_note-udayu-237)

## Race, ethnicity, and citizenship

Main articles: [Native American identity](/wiki/Native_American_identity "Native American identity") and [Native Americans in United States elections](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_United_States_elections "Native Americans in United States elections")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Sharice_Davids.jpg/250px-Sharice_Davids.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sharice_Davids.jpg)

[Sharice Davids](/wiki/Sharice_Davids "Sharice Davids") became one of the first two Native American women elected to the U.S. House of Representatives.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Portrait_of_Ben_Reifel.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Ben_Reifel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Ben_Reifel.jpg)

[Ben Reifel](/wiki/Ben_Reifel "Ben Reifel") of South Dakota, the only [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people") elected to the U.S. House of Representatives

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Deb_Haaland_official_portrait%2C_116th_congress_2.jpg/250px-Deb_Haaland_official_portrait%2C_116th_congress_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deb_Haaland_official_portrait,_116th_congress_2.jpg)

[Deb Haaland](/wiki/Deb_Haaland "Deb Haaland") became the first Native American to be appointed as the U.S. Secretary of the Interior.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Judge_Ada_Brown.png)](/wiki/File:Judge_Ada_Brown.png)

[Ada Brown](/wiki/Ada_Brown_(judge) "Ada Brown (judge)"), a citizen of the Choctaw Nation with mixed-African American heritage, nominated by President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") in 2019 to be a federal judge in Texas

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Rep._Mary_Peltola_headshot_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Rep._Mary_Peltola_headshot_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rep._Mary_Peltola_headshot_(cropped).jpg)

[Mary Peltola](/wiki/Mary_Peltola "Mary Peltola") became the first Alaska Native elected to the U.S. House of Representatives.

Native American identity is determined by the tribal community that the individual or group is seeking to identify with.[[247]](#cite_note-TallBear1-249)[[248]](#cite_note-nhpr-250) While it is common for non-Natives to consider it a racial or ethnic identity, it is considered by Native Americans in the United States to be a political identity, based on citizenship and immediate family relationships.[[247]](#cite_note-TallBear1-249)[[248]](#cite_note-nhpr-250) As culture can vary widely between the 574 extant [federally recognized tribes in the United States](/wiki/Federally_recognized_tribes_in_the_United_States "Federally recognized tribes in the United States"), the idea of a single unified "Native American" racial identity is a European construct that does not have an equivalent in tribal thought.[[247]](#cite_note-TallBear1-249)

Historically, numerous Native Americans [assimilated into colonial and later American society](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation_of_Native_Americans "Cultural assimilation of Native Americans"), e.g. through [adopting English](/wiki/Language_shift "Language shift") and [converting to Christianity](/wiki/Praying_Indians "Praying Indians"). In many cases, this process occurred through [forced assimilation](/wiki/Forced_assimilation "Forced assimilation") of children sent off to [American Indian boarding schools](/wiki/American_Indian_boarding_schools "American Indian boarding schools") far from their families. Those who could eventually [pass for white](/wiki/Racial_passing "Racial passing") gained the advantage of [white privilege](/wiki/White_privilege "White privilege"), yet often paid for it with the loss of community connections.[[249]](#cite_note-auto-251) With the enforcement of [blood quantum laws](/wiki/Blood_quantum_laws "Blood quantum laws"), Indian blood could be diluted over generations through intermarrying with non-Native populations, as well as intermarrying with members of tribes that also required high blood-quantum, solely from one tribe.[[250]](#cite_note-252) "Kill the Indian, save the man" was a mantra of nineteenth-century U.S. assimilation policies.[[251]](#cite_note-TallBear_31–66-253)

### Tribal enrollment

Further information: [List of federally recognized tribes in the United States](/wiki/List_of_federally_recognized_tribes_in_the_United_States "List of federally recognized tribes in the United States"), [State-recognized tribes](/wiki/State-recognized_tribes "State-recognized tribes"), [Cherokee freedmen controversy](/wiki/Cherokee_freedmen_controversy "Cherokee freedmen controversy"), [Cherokee descent](/wiki/Cherokee_descent "Cherokee descent"), and [Tribal disenrollment](/wiki/Tribal_disenrollment "Tribal disenrollment")

Requirements for tribal citizenship vary by tribe, but are generally based on who one's parents and grandparents are, as known and documented by community members and tribal records. Among the tribal nations, qualification for enrolling those who were not logged at birth by their parents may be based upon a required percentage of Native American "blood" (or the "[blood quantum](/wiki/Blood_quantum_laws "Blood quantum laws")") of an individual, or upon documented [lineal descent](/wiki/Lineal_descent "Lineal descent") from an ancestor on a specific census or register.

Tribal rules regarding the recognition of members who have heritage from multiple tribes also vary, but most do not allow citizenship in multiple tribes at once. For those that do, usually citizens consider one of their citizenships primary, and their other heritage to be "descent". Federally recognized tribes do not accept genetic ethnicity percentages results as appropriate evidence of Native American identity, as they cannot indicate specific tribe, or even whether or not someone is Native American. Unless requested for a paternity test, they do not advise applicants to submit such things.[[251]](#cite_note-TallBear_31–66-253)

### Increased self-identification

In the 2010 Census, nearly 3 million people indicated that their "race" was Native American (including Alaska Native).[[252]](#cite_note-254) Of these, more than 27% specifically indicated "Cherokee" as their [ethnic origin](/wiki/Ethnic_origin "Ethnic origin").[[253]](#cite_note-255)[[254]](#cite_note-256) This phenomenon has been dubbed the "[Cherokee Syndrome](/wiki/Cherokee_Syndrome "Cherokee Syndrome")".[[255]](#cite_note-257) Across the US, numerous individuals cultivate an [opportunistic ethnic identity](/wiki/Ethnic_option "Ethnic option") as Native American, sometimes through [Cherokee heritage groups](/wiki/Cherokee_heritage_groups "Cherokee heritage groups") or [Indian Wedding Blessings](/wiki/Indian_Wedding_Blessing "Indian Wedding Blessing").[[249]](#cite_note-auto-251)

Since the 2000 census, people may identify as being of more than one race.[[39]](#cite_note-2010_Census_AMAN-39) Since the 1960s, the number of people claiming Native American ancestry has grown significantly and, by the 2000 census, the number had more than doubled. Sociologists attribute this dramatic change to "ethnic shifting" or "ethnic shopping"; they believe that it reflects a willingness of people to question their birth identities and adopt new ethnicities which they find more compatible.

The author [Jack Hitt](/wiki/Jack_Hitt "Jack Hitt") writes:

> The reaction from lifelong Indians runs the gamut. It is easy to find Native Americans who denounce many of these new Indians as members of the [wannabe tribe](/wiki/Pretendian "Pretendian"). But it is also easy to find Indians like Clem Iron Wing, an elder among the [Lakota](/wiki/Lakota_people "Lakota people"), who sees this flood of new ethnic claims as magnificent, a surge of Indians 'trying to come home.' Those Indians who ridicule Iron Wing's lax sense of tribal membership have retrofitted the old genocidal system of blood quantum—measuring racial purity by blood—into the new standard for real Indianness, a choice rich with paradox.[[45]](#cite_note-Newest_Indians-45)

Journalist Mary Annette Pember ([Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe")) writes that non-Natives identifying with Native American identity may be a result of a person's increased interest in [genealogy](/wiki/Genealogy "Genealogy"), the romanticization of what they believe the cultures to be, and family lore of Native American ancestors in the distant past. However, there are different issues if a person wants to pursue enrollment as a citizen of a tribal nation. Different tribes have different requirements for citizenship. Often those who live as non-Natives, yet claim distant heritage, say they are simply reluctant to enroll, arguing that it is a method of control initiated by the federal government. However, it is the tribes that set their own enrollment criteria, and "the various enrollment requirements are often a hurdle that ethnic shoppers are unable to clear." Says Grayson Noley, (Choctaw), of the [University of Oklahoma](/wiki/University_of_Oklahoma "University of Oklahoma"), "If you have to search for proof of your heritage, it probably isn't there."[[256]](#cite_note-EthnicFraud-258) In other cases, there are some individuals who are 100% Native American but, if all of their recent ancestors are from different tribes, [blood quantum laws](/wiki/Blood_quantum_laws "Blood quantum laws") could result in them not meeting the citizenship criteria for any one of those individual tribes. Pember concludes:

> The subjects of genuine American Indian blood, cultural connection and recognition by the community are extremely contentious issues, hotly debated throughout Indian country and beyond. The whole situation, some say, is ripe for misinterpretation, confusion and, ultimately, exploitation.[[256]](#cite_note-EthnicFraud-258)

## DNA

Main article: [Genetic history of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Genetic history of the Indigenous peoples of the Americas")

The genetic history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas primarily focuses on [human Y-chromosome DNA haplogroups](/wiki/Human_Y-chromosome_DNA_haplogroup "Human Y-chromosome DNA haplogroup") and [human mitochondrial DNA haplogroups](/wiki/Human_mitochondrial_DNA_haplogroups "Human mitochondrial DNA haplogroups"). "Y-DNA" is passed solely along the [patrilineal](/wiki/Patrilineal "Patrilineal") line, from father to son, while "mtDNA" is passed down the [matrilineal](/wiki/Matrilineal "Matrilineal") line, from mother to offspring of both sexes. Neither [recombines](/wiki/Genetic_recombination "Genetic recombination"), and thus Y-DNA and mtDNA change only by chance mutation at each generation with no intermixture between parents' genetic material.[[257]](#cite_note-nomenclature-259) [Autosomal](/wiki/Autosome "Autosome") "atDNA" markers are also used, but differ from mtDNA or Y-DNA in that they overlap significantly.[[258]](#cite_note-Griffiths-260) Autosomal DNA is generally used to measure the average continent-of-ancestry [genetic admixture](/wiki/Genetic_admixture "Genetic admixture") in the entire [human genome](/wiki/Human_genome "Human genome") and related [isolated populations](/wiki/Population_bottleneck "Population bottleneck").[[258]](#cite_note-Griffiths-260) Within mtDNA, genetic scientists have found specific nucleotide sequences that they have classified as "Native American markers" because the sequences are understood to have been inherited through the generations of genetic females within populations first found in the "New World". There are five primary Native American mtDNA haplogroups in which there are clusters of closely linked markers inherited together. All five haplogroups have been identified by researchers as "prehistoric Native North American samples", and it is commonly asserted that the majority of living Native Americans possess one of the common five mtDNA haplogroup markers.[[251]](#cite_note-TallBear_31–66-253)

The genetic pattern indicates Indigenous Americans experienced two very distinctive genetic episodes; first with the initial-peopling of the Americas, and secondly with [European colonization of the Americas](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas").[[259]](#cite_note-SpencerWells3-261)[[260]](#cite_note-Genebase-262)[[261]](#cite_note-263) The former is the determinant factor for the number of [gene](/wiki/Gene "Gene") lineages, [zygosity](/wiki/Zygosity "Zygosity") mutations and founding [haplotypes](/wiki/Haplotype "Haplotype") present in today's Indigenous American [populations](/wiki/Population_history_of_Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Population history of Indigenous peoples of the Americas").[[260]](#cite_note-Genebase-262)

The most popular theory is that human settlement of the Americas occurred in stages from the [Bering sea coast line](/wiki/Bering_Sea "Bering Sea"), with an initial 15,000 to 20,000-year layover on [Beringia](/wiki/Beringia "Beringia") for the small [founding population](/wiki/Founder_effect "Founder effect").[[259]](#cite_note-SpencerWells3-261)[[262]](#cite_note-First-264)[[263]](#cite_note-first2-265) The [micro-satellite](/wiki/Microsatellite_(genetics) "Microsatellite (genetics)") diversity and distributions of the Y lineage specific to South America indicates that certain [Amerindian](/wiki/Amerindian "Amerindian") populations have been isolated since the initial colonization of the region.[[264]](#cite_note-subclades-266) The [Na-Dené](/wiki/Na-Den%C3%A9 "Na-Dené"), [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") and [Indigenous Alaskan](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives") populations exhibit [haplogroup Q-M242 (Y-DNA)](/wiki/Haplogroup_Q-M242_(Y-DNA) "Haplogroup Q-M242 (Y-DNA)") mutations, however, that are distinct from other Indigenous Amerindians, and that have various mtDNA and atDNA mutations.[[265]](#cite_note-NaDene-267)[[266]](#cite_note-Zhivotovsky-268)[[267]](#cite_note-inuit-269) This suggests that the paleo-Indian migrants into the northern extremes of North America and [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") were descended from a later, independent migrant population.[[268]](#cite_note-270)[[269]](#cite_note-Nadene1-271)

Genetic analyses of HLA I and HLA II genes as well as HLA-A, -B, and -DRB1 gene frequencies links the [Ainu people](/wiki/Ainu_people "Ainu people") of northern [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and southeastern [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") to some [Indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas"), especially to populations on the [Pacific Northwest Coast](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Pacific_Northwest_Coast "Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast") such as [Tlingit](/wiki/Tlingit "Tlingit"). Scientists suggest that the main ancestor of the Ainu and of some Native American groups can be traced back to [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") groups in [Southern Siberia](/wiki/Southern_Siberia "Southern Siberia").[[270]](#cite_note-272)
